
Ground-truth references:
- Peskin & Schroeder (1995), Appendix A: trace identities, contraction identities
- Nakahara (2003), Ch 12: spin connection, Dirac operator on curved spacetime
- Van Proeyen, "Tools for Supersymmetry": Fierz identities, sigma conventions

## 13. Key Design Decisions and Rationale

1. **Implicit Dirac indices**: Following standard physics convention. Explicit indices only when needed (trace, Fierz). This keeps expressions readable and avoids doubling the index count on every gamma matrix.

2. **Tensor flag, not new AST node**: Reuses entire existing infrastructure. The exterior calculus, spin metric, and frame bundle modules all use this pattern. Adding a new TensorExpr subtype would require modifying 20+ functions.

3. **Separate dirac_simplify pipeline**: Non-commutativity of gamma matrices is fundamentally incompatible with the generic canonicalize (which assumes commuting factors). A separate pipeline is cleaner than trying to make canonicalize non-commutativity-aware for all cases.

4. **Frame (Lorentz) indices for gamma^a**: Gamma matrices naturally carry frame indices (they are constant in a frame). This aligns with the existing frame bundle infrastructure and the standard physics convention.

5. **Recursive trace evaluation**: The recursive algorithm (strip one gamma, apply anticommutation, recurse) is both simple and general. It handles any number of gamma matrices and extends naturally to include gamma5 traces.

6. **dim as parameter, not hardcoded 4**: Supporting dimensional regularization (d = 4 - 2*epsilon) requires carrying d symbolically. The contraction identities gamma^a gamma_a = d, gamma^a gamma^b gamma_a = -(d-2) gamma^b, etc. all work for symbolic d. The Dirac bundle dimension 2^(d/2) is set at registration time for a specific integer d; for dimensional regularization, d in the contraction formulas is a parameter while the bundle dimension stays 4.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"tobias.osborne@itp.uni-hannover.de","created_at":"2026-03-14T22:11:52Z","created_by":"tobiasosborne","updated_at":"2026-03-15T17:57:47Z","closed_at":"2026-03-15T17:57:47Z","close_reason":"Implemented and tested"}

{"id":"TGR-ec6","title":"EFTofPNG: Implement Feynman diagram type hierarchy and builder API","description":"Implement the core data structures for representing Feynman diagrams in gravitational EFT.

## Background

Before implementing individual vertices and the contraction engine, we need a type hierarchy for representing n-point tensor structures in momentum space, and a builder API for assembling diagrams from vertices and propagators.

The existing codebase has KineticKernel (bilinear decomposition) in src/action/kernel_extraction.jl, QuadraticForm in src/action/quadratic_action.jl, and Worldline in src/worldline/worldline.jl. The Feynman diagram infrastructure must interoperate with all of these.

Reference: Goldberger & Rothstein hep-th/0409156, Sec 2 — general structure of NRGR Feynman rules.

## Design: Type hierarchy

```julia
# An n-point tensor vertex in momentum space
# Represents V^{(n)}_{a1b1, a2b2, ..., anbn}(k1, k2, ..., kn) with momentum conservation
struct TensorVertex
    name::Symbol                     # e.g., :V3_EH, :V4_EH, :V_matter
    index_groups::Vector{Vector{TIndex}}  # one group per leg: [[a1,b1], [a2,b2], ...]
    momenta::Vector{Symbol}          # momentum label at each leg: [:k1, :k2, :k3]
    expr::TensorExpr                 # the vertex tensor expression
    coupling_order::Int              # power of kappa (n-2 for pure graviton)
    symmetry_group::Vector{Vector{Int}}  # Bose symmetry: permutations of (leg, momentum) pairs
end

# A propagator connecting two index groups
struct TensorPropagator
    name::Symbol                     # e.g., :D_graviton, :D_scalar
    indices_left::Vector{TIndex}     # index group at one end: [a, b]
    indices_right::Vector{TIndex}    # index group at other end: [c, d]
    momentum::Symbol                 # momentum label
    expr::TensorExpr                 # the propagator tensor expression
    gauge_param::Any                 # gauge parameter (xi for R_xi gauge)
end

# A complete Feynman diagram = graph of vertices connected by propagators
struct FeynmanDiagram
    vertices::Vector{TensorVertex}
    propagators::Vector{TensorPropagator}
    topology::Vector{Tuple{Int,Int,Int,Int}}  # (vertex_a, leg_a, vertex_b, leg_b)
    external_legs::Vector{@NamedTuple{vertex::Int, leg::Int, momentum::Symbol}}
    loop_momenta::Vector{Symbol}
    symmetry_factor::Rational{Int}
end

# Result of contracting a diagram
struct DiagramAmplitude
    external_indices::Vector{Vector{TIndex}}  # free index groups
    external_momenta::Vector{Symbol}          # external momentum labels
    expr::TensorExpr                          # contracted tensor expression (tree)
    loop_integrals::Vector{Any}               # MomentumIntegral objects (loop, from TGR-xex)
end
```

## Builder API function signatures

```julia
# Create a vertex from the perturbation engine output
function vertex_from_perturbation(mp::MetricPerturbation, order::Int;
                                   fourier::Bool=true,
                                   index_groups=nothing) -> TensorVertex

# Create a matter vertex from a worldline
function vertex_from_worldline(wl::Worldline, order::Int;
                                kappa::Symbol=:kappa) -> TensorVertex

# Combine vertices and propagators into a diagram
function build_diagram(vertices::Vector{TensorVertex},
                       propagators::Vector{TensorPropagator},
                       connections::Vector{Tuple{Int,Int,Int,Int}};
                       external_momenta::Vector{Symbol}=Symbol[]) -> FeynmanDiagram

# Standard tree-level diagrams (convenience constructors)
function tree_exchange_diagram(v1::TensorVertex, v2::TensorVertex,
                               prop::TensorPropagator;
                               external_momenta=[:k]) -> FeynmanDiagram

# s-channel, t-channel, u-channel diagrams for 2-to-2 scattering
function scattering_diagrams_stu(v3::TensorVertex, prop::TensorPropagator;
                                  external_momenta=[:k1,:k2,:k3,:k4]) -> Vector{FeynmanDiagram}

# Compute the Euler characteristic and loop count
function n_loops(diagram::FeynmanDiagram) -> Int
# L = E - V + 1 (E = edges, V = vertices, connected)
```

## Integration with existing infrastructure

- TensorExpr AST from src/types.jl — vertex expressions are TensorExpr trees
- TIndex, up, down, fresh_index from src/ast/indices.jl — index management
- ensure_no_dummy_clash — critical for combining vertices/propagators without index collisions
- expand_perturbation from src/perturbation/expand.jl — generates vertex expressions
- to_fourier from src/svt/fourier.jl — momentum space transformation
- TensorRegistry, register_tensor! — register vertex tensors with symmetries

## Ground truth (verification tests)

1. Type construction: TensorVertex and TensorPropagator constructors compile. A 3-graviton vertex with 3 index groups of 2 indices each has 6 total free indices.

2. Diagram topology: build_diagram for a single-exchange tree diagram (2 vertices, 1 propagator) reports n_loops = 0. A bubble diagram (2 vertices, 2 propagators) reports n_loops = 1.

3. Index consistency: build_diagram verifies that each internal connection pairs index groups of compatible rank (both rank-2 for graviton lines).

4. Momentum conservation: At each vertex, sum of incoming momenta = 0. For a tree exchange with vertices at k and -k, the propagator carries momentum k.

## Implementation notes

- Place in src/feynman/types.jl (new directory src/feynman/)
- ~120 LOC for type definitions and builder API
- This issue provides the structural foundation; TGR-vzx, TGR-qks, TGR-9vn populate the types with physics
- Must export: TensorVertex, TensorPropagator, FeynmanDiagram, DiagramAmplitude, build_diagram","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tobias.osborne@itp.uni-hannover.de","created_at":"2026-03-15T10:49:47Z","created_by":"tobiasosborne","updated_at":"2026-03-15T16:30:22Z","closed_at":"2026-03-15T16:30:22Z","close_reason":"Implemented and merged"}

{"id":"TGR-oun","title":"Spinors: Register SL2C and SL2C_dot VBundles with conjugation metadata","description":"## Summary

Register two new VBundles on the spacetime manifold for spinor indices:
- :SL2C (undotted/unprimed spinor bundle, dim=2, indices [:A,:B,:C,:D,:E,:F])
- :SL2C_dot (dotted/primed spinor bundle, dim=2, indices [:Ap,:Bp,:Cp,:Dp,:Ep,:Fp])

Add a :conjugate_bundle field to VBundleProperties.options linking :SL2C <-> :SL2C_dot.
This tells the contraction and canonicalization engines that these two bundles are complex
conjugates: a tensor with indices from both bundles is a mixed spinor.

## Design Rationale (from TGR-7im research)

Three options were evaluated for encoding dotted/undotted distinction:

1. **Extend IndexPosition** (SpinorUp/SpinorDown/DottedUp/DottedDown): Breaks all
   existing code that switches on Up/Down. Every function touching IndexPosition must
   be updated. Rejected.

2. **New SpinorIndex type**: Requires parallel code paths everywhere (indices(),
   free_indices(), rename_dummy(), canonicalize()). Massive code duplication. Rejected.

3. **VBundle approach** (CHOSEN): TIndex already carries vbundle::Symbol. Create
   :SL2C and :SL2C_dot bundles. The existing infrastructure already:
   - Groups indices by (name, vbundle) in _analyze_indices()
   - Checks vbundle match in contraction engine (contraction.jl line 136, 207)
   - Handles non-Tangent vbundles (define_vbundle! already exists)
   No core type changes needed. Backward compatible.

The conjugation relationship is stored as metadata on VBundleProperties via an
options dict or new field, NOT by encoding it in the index name (primes cause
ParseError in Julia).

## Functions

```julia
# In src/spinors/bundles.jl (~60 LOC)

define_spinor_bundles!(reg::TensorRegistry; manifold::Symbol=:M4)
```

Registers:
1. VBundle :SL2C on manifold, dim=2, indices=[:A,:B,:C,:D,:E,:F]
2. VBundle :SL2C_dot on manifold, dim=2, indices=[:Ap,:Bp,:Cp,:Dp,:Ep,:Fp]
3. Sets conjugation metadata: reg.vbundles[:SL2C].options[:conjugate] = :SL2C_dot
   (requires adding options::Dict{Symbol,Any} to VBundleProperties, or using
   a registry-level conjugation cache Dict{Symbol,Symbol})

## Convenience constructors

```julia
spin_up(s::Symbol) = TIndex(s, Up, :SL2C)
spin_down(s::Symbol) = TIndex(s, Down, :SL2C)
spin_dot_up(s::Symbol) = TIndex(s, Up, :SL2C_dot)
spin_dot_down(s::Symbol) = TIndex(s, Down, :SL2C_dot)
```

Up/Down still means contravariant/covariant (raised/lowered by spin metric).
The dotted/undotted distinction is carried by the vbundle, not by position.

## VBundleProperties change

Add options dict to VBundleProperties (currently has: name, manifold, dim, indices).
Options: {:conjugate => :SL2C_dot, :is_spinor => true}. This is a BREAKING change
to VBundleProperties struct -- use kwarg constructor with defaults for backward compat.

## Ground truth

- Penrose & Rindler, "Spinors and Space-Time" Vol 1 (1984), Section 2.5
- Wald, "General Relativity" (1984), Chapter 13
- xAct/Spinors package uses VBundle approach with conjugation flag

## Tests

1. define_spinor_bundles! registers both bundles on manifold
2. spin_up(:A).vbundle == :SL2C
3. spin_dot_down(:Ap).vbundle == :SL2C_dot
4. Conjugation metadata round-trip: conjugate of :SL2C is :SL2C_dot and vice versa
5. TIndex(:A, Up, :SL2C) != TIndex(:A, Up, :SL2C_dot) (different vbundles)
6. Backward compat: existing VBundleProperties(...) 4-arg constructor still works","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tobias.osborne@itp.uni-hannover.de","created_at":"2026-03-15T10:20:17Z","created_by":"tobiasosborne","updated_at":"2026-03-15T10:37:49Z","closed_at":"2026-03-15T10:37:49Z","close_reason":"Implemented with Penrose-Rindler Vol 1 ground truth"}

{"id":"TGR-ozc","title":"Spinors: Implement soldering form sigma^a_{AA'} and tensor-spinor conversion","description":"## Summary

Implement the soldering form (Infeld-van der Waerden symbols) sigma^a_{AA'} that
provides the isomorphism between the tangent bundle and the tensor product of the
two spin bundles: V_a <-> V_{AA'} via V_{AA'} = sigma^a_{AA'} V_a.

The soldering form has 3 types of indices: one :Tangent (Up), one :SL2C (Down),
one :SL2C_dot (Down). It satisfies fundamental identities that encode the metric
and spin metric in terms of each other.

## Tensor registration

```julia
# In src/spinors/soldering.jl (~120 LOC)

define_soldering_form!(reg::TensorRegistry; manifold::Symbol=:M4, metric::Symbol=:g)
```

Registers :sigma with mixed indices:
- rank specification needs extension: currently rank=(p,q) counts Up/Down on Tangent.
  For mixed vbundle tensors, rank should be a Dict or the existing (p,q) extended.
  DECISION: Keep rank=(1,2) with options[:index_vbundles] = [:Tangent, :SL2C, :SL2C_dot]
  specifying the vbundle of each slot. This metadata is needed by canonicalize.

The soldering form has no slot symmetries (sigma^a_{AA'} != sigma^a_{A'A} because
the two down indices are on DIFFERENT vbundles and thus not interchangeable).

## Conversion functions

```julia
# tensor_to_spinor(expr, idx::Symbol; sigma=:sigma) -> TensorExpr
# Replace tensor index :a with spinor pair :AA' by inserting sigma:
#   T_a -> sigma^a_{AA'} T_a  (contracts tensor index, introduces spinor pair)
#   More precisely: T_{...a...} -> sigma^a_{AA'} T_{...a...}
#                                 = T_{...AA'...} (after contraction)

# spinor_to_tensor(expr, idx_undotted::Symbol, idx_dotted::Symbol; sigma=:sigma)
# Replace spinor pair (A, A') with tensor index by inserting sigma inverse:
#   T_{AA'} -> sigma_{AA'}^a T_{AA'} = T_a

# spinor_equivalent(expr; sigma=:sigma) -> TensorExpr
# Convert ALL tensor indices to spinor pairs. E.g.:
#   g_{ab} -> sigma^a_{AA'} sigma^b_{BB'} g_{ab} = epsilon_{AB} epsilon_{A'B'}
```

## Key identities (rules to register)

1. sigma^a_{AA'} sigma_{a}^{BB'} = epsilon^B_A epsilon^{B'}_{A'} = delta^B_A delta^{B'}_{A'}
   (completeness on spinor side)
2. sigma^a_{AA'} sigma^{b AA'} = g^{ab}  (equivalently: delta^a_b on tensor side)
3. g_{ab} = sigma_a^{AA'} sigma_{b AA'}  (metric from soldering form)
4. epsilon_{AB} epsilon_{A'B'} = sigma^a_{AA'} sigma_{a BB'}  (spin metrics from soldering)

## Ground truth

- Penrose & Rindler Vol 1 (1984), Eq 3.1.20, 3.1.23, 3.1.39
- Wald Ch 13, Eq 13.1.17-13.1.22
- Convention: sigma^a_{AA'} (tensor index up, both spinor indices down)
  with sigma_{a}^{AA'} obtained by raising/lowering

## Tests

1. sigma^a_{AA'} sigma_{a}^{BB'} simplifies to delta^B_A delta^{B'}_{A'}
2. sigma^a_{AA'} sigma^{b AA'} simplifies to g^{ab} (or delta^a_b)
3. tensor_to_spinor of V_a produces expression with spinor indices A, A'
4. spinor_to_tensor inverts tensor_to_spinor (round-trip identity)
5. Index counting: sigma has 1 Tangent + 1 SL2C + 1 SL2C_dot = 3 total slots","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tobias.osborne@itp.uni-hannover.de","created_at":"2026-03-15T10:22:04Z","created_by":"tobiasosborne","updated_at":"2026-03-15T17:57:42Z","closed_at":"2026-03-15T17:57:42Z","close_reason":"Implemented and tested"}

{"id":"TGR-qks","title":"EFTofPNG: Implement 3-point and 4-point graviton vertices from expanded Einstein-Hilbert","description":"Implement 3-point and 4-point graviton vertices from the expanded Einstein-Hilbert action.

## Background

The EH action S_EH = (1/(2 kappa^2)) int d^d x sqrt(-g) R, expanded in h_{ab} = g_{ab} - eta_{ab} with kappa = sqrt(32 pi G), yields an infinite tower of n-graviton vertices. The cubic and quartic vertices are needed for 1PN (tree-level) and 2PN (1-loop) computations.

References:
- DeWitt, Phys. Rev. 162 (1967) 1195, Sec III — original 3-vertex derivation
- Sannan, PRD 34 (1986) 1749, Eq 3.3 — explicit 3-graviton vertex in de Donder gauge
- Goldberger & Rothstein, hep-th/0409156, Sec 2 — EFT context
- Porto, 1104.2712, Sec 2.1 — NRGR vertex rules

## Derivation via existing perturbation engine

The codebase already has arbitrary-order metric perturbation expansion:
- `expand_perturbation(expr, mp, order)` in `src/perturbation/expand.jl`
- `delta_christoffel`, `delta_riemann`, `delta_ricci`, `delta_ricci_scalar`
- `to_fourier` in `src/svt/fourier.jl` for momentum space

The 3-vertex is obtained from delta^3 S_EH = (1/(2 kappa^2)) int d^d x [delta^3(sqrt(-g) R)], which involves:
- delta^3(sqrt(-g)) = sqrt(-g) * [(1/2)h delta^2 h - (1/8) h^3 + ...]
- delta^1 R * delta^2(sqrt(-g)) + delta^2 R * delta^1(sqrt(-g)) + delta^3 R

Pipeline: define_metric_perturbation! -> expand_perturbation at order 3 -> to_fourier

## Function signatures

```julia
# 3-graviton vertex: V^(3)(h1_{a1 b1}(k1), h2_{a2 b2}(k2), h3_{a3 b3}(k3))
# Returns TensorExpr with 6 free indices + 3 momentum vectors
graviton_vertex_3(a1::TIndex, b1::TIndex, a2::TIndex, b2::TIndex,
                  a3::TIndex, b3::TIndex;
                  k_names::NTuple{3,Symbol}=(:k1,:k2,:k3),
                  metric::Symbol=:eta, kappa::Symbol=:kappa) -> TensorExpr

# 4-graviton vertex: V^(4) with 8 free indices + 4 momenta
graviton_vertex_4(indices::NTuple{8,TIndex};
                  k_names::NTuple{4,Symbol}=(:k1,:k2,:k3,:k4),
                  metric::Symbol=:eta, kappa::Symbol=:kappa) -> TensorExpr

# Generic n-point vertex via expand_perturbation
graviton_vertex_n(n::Int, index_groups::Vector{Tuple{TIndex,TIndex}};
                  ...) -> TensorExpr
```

## Ground truth (verification tests)

1. **Sannan 3-vertex**: The 3-graviton vertex in momentum space (de Donder gauge) has the schematic form V^(3)_{a1b1,a2b2,a3b3}(k1,k2,k3) with momentum conservation k1+k2+k3=0. Verify against Sannan PRD 34 (1986) Eq 3.3 — the vertex has 12 independent tensor structures (after imposing Bose symmetry under permutation of (index pair, momentum)).

2. **On-shell 3-point amplitude**: Contract with 3 on-shell graviton polarizations epsilon^{ab}_i (transverse: k_a epsilon^{ab} = 0, traceless: eta_{ab} epsilon^{ab} = 0, massless: k^2 = 0). Result must have the spinor-helicity structure M_3 ~ kappa * [12]^2 [23]^2 [31]^2 / (k1.k2 k2.k3 k3.k1) (schematic). Reference: Elvang & Huang, "Scattering Amplitudes" (2015), Ch 2.

3. **NRGR limit**: In the NRGR limit (k0 << |k|), the temporal-component vertex V^(3)_{00,00,00} reduces to the Newtonian 3-body vertex. Reference: Goldberger & Rothstein hep-th/0409156 Eq. 7.

4. **Bose symmetry**: V^(3) is invariant under simultaneous permutation of (index group, momentum): V_{a1b1,a2b2,a3b3}(k1,k2,k3) = V_{a2b2,a1b1,a3b3}(k2,k1,k3).

## Implementation notes

- Use expand_perturbation at order n, not hand-coded formulas
- Factor out kappa^{n-2} coupling constant
- Fourier transform each derivative -> momentum via to_fourier
- For NRGR: implement a time-space split of the vertex, interfacing with foliation infrastructure
- ~150 LOC in src/feynman/vertices.jl","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tobias.osborne@itp.uni-hannover.de","created_at":"2026-03-14T22:31:23Z","created_by":"tobiasosborne","updated_at":"2026-03-15T16:55:06Z","closed_at":"2026-03-15T16:55:06Z","close_reason":"Implemented with DeWitt/Sannan ground truth, 65 tests"}

New directory: src/phase_space/.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","owner":"tobias.osborne@itp.uni-hannover.de","created_at":"2026-03-14T22:11:10Z","created_by":"tobiasosborne","updated_at":"2026-03-15T16:42:24Z","closed_at":"2026-03-15T16:42:24Z","close_reason":"all steps complete"}

{"id":"TGR-s50.1","title":"Research: Design covariant phase space framework for TensorGR.jl","description":"Design Wald's covariant phase space formalism for TensorGR.jl.

## Completed Design

### Physics Overview (Iyer-Wald 1994, Wald 1990, Lee-Wald 1990)

The covariant phase space (CPS) formalism constructs the symplectic structure of a
field theory directly from the Lagrangian, without choosing a 3+1 decomposition.

The pipeline proceeds through these objects:

  L[phi]  -->  (E, Theta)  -->  omega  -->  J  -->  Q  -->  entropy/charges

1. **Lagrangian L**: A d-form on spacetime depending on dynamical fields phi.
2. **EOM + Symplectic potential**: delta L = E*delta_phi + d Theta. E=0 are EOM;
   Theta is a (d-1)-form on spacetime, 1-form on field space.
3. **Symplectic current**: omega = delta_1 Theta[delta_2] - delta_2 Theta[delta_1].
   A (d-1)-form, bilinear antisymmetric in field variations.
4. **Noether current**: J = Theta(Lie_xi phi) - xi.L for vector field xi.
   Conserved on-shell: dJ = 0.
5. **Noether charge**: J = E*Lie_xi phi + dQ on-shell. Q is a (d-2)-form.
6. **Conserved charges**: H_xi = integral_{dSigma} Q (mass, angular momentum).
7. **Wald entropy**: S = -2pi integral_H E_R^{abcd} epsilon_ab epsilon_cd.

### Type Hierarchy (new directory: src/phase_space/)

```
LagrangianDensity
  .expr::TensorExpr, .fields::Vector{Symbol}
  .metric::Symbol, .covd::Symbol, .dim::Int

SymplecticPotential
  .expr::TensorExpr (Theta^mu), .field, .delta_field, .lagrangian

SymplecticCurrent
  .expr::TensorExpr (omega^mu), .delta1_field, .delta2_field, .potential

NoetherCurrent
  .expr::TensorExpr (J^mu), .xi::Symbol, .lagrangian, .potential

NoetherCharge
  .expr::TensorExpr (Q^{mu nu}), .xi::Symbol, .lagrangian, .current
```

### Data Flow

```
LagrangianDensity --[eom_extract]--> (E, Theta::SymplecticPotential)
SymplecticPotential --[symplectic_current]--> omega::SymplecticCurrent
(SymplecticPotential, xi) --[noether_current]--> J::NoetherCurrent
NoetherCurrent --[noether_charge]--> Q::NoetherCharge
  (uses: is_divergence + extract_potential from TGR-s50.7/s50.8)
LagrangianDensity --[riemann_derivative]--> dL/dR (for Iyer-Wald formula)
(NoetherCharge, epsilon) --[wald_entropy]--> S (scalar)
```

### API Surface

```julia
# Core pipeline
eom_extract(L::LagrangianDensity, field) -> (E, Theta)
symplectic_potential(L, field) -> SymplecticPotential
symplectic_current(Theta, delta1, delta2) -> SymplecticCurrent
noether_current(L, field, xi) -> NoetherCurrent
noether_charge(J::NoetherCurrent) -> NoetherCharge

# Utilities
is_divergence(expr, div_index) -> Bool
extract_potential(expr, div_index) -> TensorExpr
riemann_derivative(L) -> TensorExpr  (dL/dR_{abcd})

# Known formulas (EH shortcuts)
theta_eh(metric, delta_metric, covd) -> TensorExpr
noether_current_eh(metric, xi, covd) -> TensorExpr
noether_charge_eh(metric, xi, covd) -> TensorExpr

# Wald entropy
wald_entropy_integrand(L) -> TensorExpr
wald_entropy(L) -> TensorExpr

# Ambiguity analysis
add_boundary_ambiguity(Theta, Y) -> SymplecticPotential
```

### Reusable Components from Existing Codebase

| Existing function | Used by | File |
|---|---|---|
| variational_derivative | eom_extract | src/perturbation/variation.jl |
| metric_variation | eom_extract (metric field) | src/perturbation/variation.jl |
| ibp_product | boundary term tracking | src/algebra/ibp.jl |
| expand_derivatives | Leibniz rule | src/algebra/derivatives.jl |
| lie_derivative | noether_current | src/gr/lie.jl |
| interior_product | xi.L contraction | src/exterior/operations.jl |
| define_killing! | test infrastructure | src/gr/killing.jl |
| contract_curvature | simplify Q | src/algebra/contraction.jl |
| canonicalize | canonical form comparison | src/algebra/canonicalize.jl |
| expand_perturbation | delta_i(Theta) | src/perturbation/expand.jl |

### Dependency Graph (9 implementation issues)

Two parallel tracks merge at the Noether charge:

Track A (symplectic):
  s50.2 (EOM+Theta) -> s50.3 (Theta wrapper) -> s50.4 (omega)
                                               -> s50.5 (J)

Track B (divergence tools):
  s50.2 -> s50.7 (is_divergence) -> s50.8 (extract_potential)

Merge point:
  s50.5 + s50.8 -> s50.6 (Q = Noether charge)

Validation:
  s50.4 + s50.6 -> s50.9 (EH validation: Q = Komar)
  s50.9 -> s50.10 (Wald entropy: S = A/4)

### LOC Estimates

| Issue | File | LOC |
|---|---|---|
| s50.2 | src/phase_space/eom_extract.jl | ~120 |

{"id":"TGR-s50.2","title":"xCPS: Implement equations of motion extraction from Lagrangian","description":"Implement extraction of equations of motion E and symplectic potential Theta from a Lagrangian L.

## Physics (Iyer-Wald 1994, Eq 2.4; Wald 1990, Eq 2.1)

The variation of a diffeomorphism-covariant Lagrangian d-form L splits as:

  delta L = E_phi * delta_phi + d Theta(phi, delta_phi)

where E_phi = 0 are the equations of motion, and Theta is the symplectic potential
(d-1)-form. The key computational step is performing integration by parts to move
all derivatives off delta_phi, collecting the total divergence terms into Theta.

For Einstein-Hilbert L = (1/16piG) R sqrt(-g):

  E^{mu nu} = (1/16piG) G^{mu nu}  (Einstein tensor)
  Theta^mu = (1/16piG) g^{alpha beta}(nabla_mu delta_g_{alpha beta} - nabla_alpha delta_g_{mu beta})

This is also written as Theta^mu = (1/16piG)(g^{ac}g^{bd} - g^{ab}g^{cd}) nabla_b delta_g_{cd} n_a
where n_a is the surface normal.

## Implementation (~120 LOC in src/phase_space/eom_extract.jl)

### Data structures

```julia
struct LagrangianDensity
    expr::TensorExpr      # the Lagrangian scalar density
    fields::Vector{Symbol} # dynamical fields (e.g., [:g] for pure gravity)
    metric::Symbol         # background metric name
    covd::Symbol           # covariant derivative name
    dim::Int               # spacetime dimension
end
```

### Functions

```julia
eom_extract(L::LagrangianDensity, field::Symbol) -> (E::TensorExpr, Theta::TensorExpr)
```

Algorithm:
1. Call variational_derivative(L.expr, field) to get E (reuse existing)
2. Compute delta_L = the full variation including boundary terms
3. Subtract E * delta_field from delta_L
4. The remainder is nabla_mu Theta^mu -- extract Theta^mu by reading off the coefficient of the divergence
5. For metric variations, use metric_variation() to handle delta(g^{ab}) vs delta(g_{ab})

### Reuse from existing code

- variational_derivative (src/perturbation/variation.jl) -- already does EL extraction
- metric_variation -- handles delta(g_{ab}) and delta(g^{ab})
- ibp_product (src/algebra/ibp.jl) -- integration by parts
- expand_derivatives (src/algebra/derivatives.jl) -- Leibniz rule expansion

The main new work is: (a) tracking boundary terms that ibp discards, and (b) wrapping everything in the LagrangianDensity type.

## Ground truth

- Wald (1990), J. Math. Phys. 31, 2378, Eq 2.1
- Iyer & Wald (1994), PRD 50, 846, Eq 2.4
- Lee & Wald (1990), J. Math. Phys. 31, 725, Sec II

## Acceptance criteria

1. eom_extract(L_EH, :g) returns (G_{mu nu}/(16piG), Theta_EH^mu)
2. Theta_EH matches the known formula: (1/16piG)(g^{ac}g^{bd} - g^{ab}g^{cd}) nabla_b delta_g_{cd}
3. For scalar field L = (1/2) g^{ab} partial_a phi partial_b phi, E = -Box phi and Theta^mu = partial^mu phi * delta_phi

{"id":"TGR-s50.3","title":"xCPS: Implement symplectic potential Θ","description":"Implement the symplectic potential (d-1)-form Theta^mu[phi, delta_phi].

## Physics (Iyer-Wald 1994, Eq 2.5-2.6; Lee-Wald 1990, Eq 2.2)

The symplectic potential Theta is the boundary term extracted from the variation of L:

  delta L = E * delta_phi + nabla_mu Theta^mu(phi, delta_phi)

Theta^mu is a vector density (equivalently, a (d-1)-form on spacetime) that is linear
in the field variation delta_phi and its derivatives. It is defined up to an ambiguity:

  Theta -> Theta + d Y(phi, delta_phi) + W(phi) * delta_phi

where Y is an exact (d-2)-form and W vanishes on-shell.

For EH gravity (Iyer-Wald Eq 3.3):
  Theta^mu[g, delta_g] = (1/16piG) g^{alpha beta}(nabla^mu delta_g_{alpha beta} - nabla_alpha delta_g^mu_{beta})
                        = (1/16piG) (g^{mu[alpha} g^{beta]gamma} + g^{gamma[alpha} g^{beta]mu}) nabla_gamma delta_g_{alpha beta}

Key point: Theta depends on delta_g_{ab} AND its first derivative nabla_c delta_g_{ab}.
For higher-derivative Lagrangians (e.g., R^2, R_{ab}R^{ab}), Theta will involve higher
derivatives of delta_g.

## Implementation (~80 LOC in src/phase_space/symplectic_potential.jl)

### Data structures

```julia
struct SymplecticPotential
    expr::TensorExpr        # Theta^mu as a TensorExpr with one free upper index
    field::Symbol           # the dynamical field
    delta_field::Symbol     # symbol for the variation (e.g., :delta_g)
    lagrangian::LagrangianDensity
end
```

### Functions

```julia
symplectic_potential(L::LagrangianDensity, field::Symbol;
                     delta_field::Symbol=Symbol(:delta_, field)) -> SymplecticPotential
```

Algorithm:
1. Call eom_extract(L, field) to get (E, Theta_raw)
2. Wrap Theta_raw in SymplecticPotential struct with metadata
3. Optionally simplify Theta using registry rules

```julia
# Convenience: known Theta for standard Lagrangians
theta_eh(metric::Symbol, delta_metric::Symbol, covd::Symbol) -> TensorExpr
```

Returns the explicit EH symplectic potential without computing it from scratch.

### Ambiguity handling

```julia
add_boundary_ambiguity(Theta::SymplecticPotential, Y::TensorExpr) -> SymplecticPotential
```

Adds d Y to Theta, useful for comparing different conventions.

## Ground truth

- Iyer & Wald (1994), PRD 50, 846, Eqs 2.5-2.6, 3.3
- Lee & Wald (1990), J. Math. Phys. 31, 725, Eq 2.2
- Margalef-Bentabol & Villasenor (2021), PRD 103, 025011, Sec IV

## Acceptance criteria

1. symplectic_potential(L_EH, :g) matches known EH formula
2. Theta is linear in delta_g: Theta[alpha * delta_g] = alpha * Theta[delta_g]
3. For L = R + alpha R^2 (Gauss-Bonnet): Theta has additional terms proportional to alpha
4. add_boundary_ambiguity produces equivalent physical results

{"id":"TGR-s50.4","title":"xCPS: Implement symplectic current ω","description":"Implement the symplectic current (d-1)-form omega^mu[delta_1 phi, delta_2 phi].

## Physics (Iyer-Wald 1994, Eq 2.7; Lee-Wald 1990, Eq 2.3)

The symplectic current is the antisymmetrized second variation of the Lagrangian:

  omega^mu(phi; delta_1 phi, delta_2 phi) = delta_1 Theta^mu(phi, delta_2 phi)
                                            - delta_2 Theta^mu(phi, delta_1 phi)

Key properties:
- omega is bilinear and antisymmetric in (delta_1 phi, delta_2 phi)
- On-shell (when both variations satisfy linearized EOM): nabla_mu omega^mu = 0
- The symplectic form Omega = integral_Sigma omega^mu dSigma_mu is independent of
  the Cauchy surface Sigma (when on-shell)
- Omega defines the presymplectic structure on the covariant phase space

For EH gravity (Wald 1990, Eq 3.5):
  omega^mu = (1/16piG) P^{mu alpha beta gamma delta epsilon}
             (delta_2 g_{beta gamma} nabla_alpha delta_1 g_{delta epsilon}
              - delta_1 g_{beta gamma} nabla_alpha delta_2 g_{delta epsilon})

where P^{mu alpha beta gamma delta epsilon} = g^{mu epsilon} g^{alpha beta} g^{gamma delta}
- (1/2) g^{mu beta} g^{alpha epsilon} g^{gamma delta} - (1/2) g^{mu alpha} g^{beta gamma} g^{delta epsilon}
- (1/2) g^{beta gamma} g^{mu delta} g^{alpha epsilon} + (1/2) g^{beta gamma} g^{mu epsilon} g^{alpha delta}.

## Implementation (~80 LOC in src/phase_space/symplectic_current.jl)

### Data structures

```julia
struct SymplecticCurrent
    expr::TensorExpr        # omega^mu with one free upper index
    delta1_field::Symbol    # first variation
    delta2_field::Symbol    # second variation
    potential::SymplecticPotential
end
```

### Functions

```julia
symplectic_current(Theta::SymplecticPotential,
                   delta1::Symbol, delta2::Symbol) -> SymplecticCurrent
```

Algorithm:
1. Compute delta_1(Theta[delta_2]) by substituting delta_2 for the field variation
   in Theta, then varying the background fields with respect to delta_1
2. Compute delta_2(Theta[delta_1]) similarly
3. Return omega = step1 - step2

The variation delta_i(Theta) is computed using the existing perturbation infrastructure:
treat Theta as a functional of the background fields, and compute its first-order
perturbation with delta_i as the perturbation parameter.

```julia
# Verify on-shell conservation
verify_omega_conservation(omega::SymplecticCurrent; registry) -> Bool
```

## Ground truth

- Iyer & Wald (1994), PRD 50, 846, Eq 2.7
- Lee & Wald (1990), J. Math. Phys. 31, 725, Eq 2.3
- Wald (1990), J. Math. Phys. 31, 2378, Eq 3.5

## Acceptance criteria

1. omega[delta_1, delta_2] = -omega[delta_2, delta_1] (antisymmetry)
2. omega is bilinear: omega[alpha*delta_1, delta_2] = alpha * omega[delta_1, delta_2]
3. For EH: omega matches the known P-tensor formula above
4. nabla_mu omega^mu simplifies to zero when both variations satisfy linearized Einstein

{"id":"TGR-s50.5","title":"xCPS: Implement Noether current J","description":"Implement the Noether current (d-1)-form J^mu[xi] for diffeomorphisms.

## Physics (Iyer-Wald 1994, Eq 2.10-2.12; Wald 1990, Eq 2.6)

For a diffeomorphism generated by vector field xi, the Noether current is:

  J^mu = Theta^mu(phi, Lie_xi phi) - xi^mu L

where Lie_xi phi is the Lie derivative of all dynamical fields along xi.

Key identity (Iyer-Wald Eq 2.12):

  J^mu = E_phi * Lie_xi phi + nabla_nu Q^{mu nu}    (*)

This means J^mu = dQ on-shell (when E = 0). The on-shell conservation nabla_mu J^mu = 0
follows from diffeomorphism invariance of L.

For a Killing vector (Lie_xi g = 0), the EH Noether current simplifies to:

  J^mu = 2 G^{mu nu} xi_nu + 2 nabla_nu nabla^{[mu} xi^{nu]}

The first term vanishes on-shell (Einstein equations), leaving J = dQ.

For EH gravity specifically:
  J^mu = (1/16piG) [2 G^{mu nu} xi_nu + nabla_nu (nabla^mu xi^nu - nabla^nu xi^mu)]

## Implementation (~100 LOC in src/phase_space/noether_current.jl)

### Data structures

```julia
struct NoetherCurrent
    expr::TensorExpr        # J^mu with one free upper index
    xi::Symbol              # the vector field generating the diffeomorphism
    lagrangian::LagrangianDensity
    potential::SymplecticPotential
end
```

### Functions

```julia
noether_current(L::LagrangianDensity, field::Symbol, xi::Symbol) -> NoetherCurrent
```

Algorithm:
1. Compute Lie_xi(field) using existing lie_derivative infrastructure
2. Evaluate Theta^mu(phi, Lie_xi phi) by substituting Lie_xi phi for delta_phi in Theta
3. Compute xi^mu * L (contraction of xi with the Lagrangian volume form)
4. Return J^mu = Theta^mu(Lie_xi phi) - xi^mu L

```julia
# Convenience: known J for EH + Killing vector
noether_current_eh(metric::Symbol, xi::Symbol, covd::Symbol) -> TensorExpr
```

```julia
# Verify on-shell conservation
verify_noether_conservation(J::NoetherCurrent; registry) -> Bool
```

### Reuse from existing code

- lie_derivative(v, expr) (src/gr/lie.jl) -- Lie derivative of tensor expressions
- define_killing! (src/gr/killing.jl) -- for testing with Killing vectors
- interior_product(v, alpha) (src/exterior/operations.jl) -- xi contracted with L

## Ground truth

- Iyer & Wald (1994), PRD 50, 846, Eqs 2.10-2.12
- Wald (1990), J. Math. Phys. 31, 2378, Eq 2.6
- Lee & Wald (1990), J. Math. Phys. 31, 725, Sec III

## Acceptance criteria

1. For EH + Killing xi: J^mu = 2 G^{mu nu} xi_nu + 2 nabla_nu nabla^{[mu} xi^{nu]}
2. On-shell (G=0): J^mu reduces to pure divergence nabla_nu(nabla^mu xi^nu - nabla^nu xi^mu)
3. nabla_mu J^mu = 0 verified symbolically (off-shell identity for diffeo-covariant L)
4. For scalar field with Killing xi: J^mu = T^{mu nu} xi_nu (stress-energy contracted with xi)

{"id":"TGR-s50.6","title":"xCPS: Implement Noether charge Q","description":"Implement the Noether charge (d-2)-form Q^{mu nu}[xi].

## Physics (Iyer-Wald 1994, Eqs 2.12, 3.1-3.2, 3.6)

The Noether charge Q^{mu nu} is defined by the decomposition:

  J^mu = E * Lie_xi phi + nabla_nu Q^{mu nu}

where J is the Noether current and E are the equations of motion. On-shell (E=0),
J^mu = nabla_nu Q^{mu nu}, so Q is the potential for the conserved current.

Q^{mu nu} is antisymmetric: Q^{mu nu} = -Q^{nu mu} (it is a (d-2)-form).

For the EH Lagrangian L = (1/16piG) R (Iyer-Wald Eq 3.6):

  Q^{mu nu} = -(1/16piG) nabla^{[mu} xi^{nu]}
             = -(1/16piG) (1/2)(nabla^mu xi^nu - nabla^nu xi^mu)

This is (up to normalization) the Komar integrand. Integrating over a (d-2)-sphere:

  H_xi = integral_{partial Sigma} Q^{mu nu} dS_{mu nu}

gives the Komar mass (for timelike Killing xi) or Komar angular momentum (for rotational xi).

For general diffeomorphism-covariant Lagrangians L(g, R, nabla R, ...),
Iyer-Wald Eq 3.2 gives:

  Q^{mu nu} = -E_R^{mu nu alpha beta} nabla_alpha xi_beta
              + 2 xi_beta nabla_alpha E_R^{mu nu alpha beta} + ...

where E_R^{mu nu alpha beta} = partial L / partial R_{mu nu alpha beta}.

## Implementation (~120 LOC in src/phase_space/noether_charge.jl)

### Data structures

```julia
struct NoetherCharge
    expr::TensorExpr        # Q^{mu nu} with two free antisymmetric upper indices
    xi::Symbol              # the diffeomorphism generator
    lagrangian::LagrangianDensity
    current::NoetherCurrent
end
```

### Functions

```julia
noether_charge(J::NoetherCurrent) -> NoetherCharge
```

Algorithm (potential extraction from divergence):
1. Given J^mu = E * Lie_xi phi + nabla_nu Q^{mu nu}, and knowing E and J
2. Compute J^mu - E * Lie_xi phi = nabla_nu Q^{mu nu}
3. Use the divergence potential extraction (TGR-s50.7/TGR-s50.8) to find Q^{mu nu}

```julia
# Direct construction for known Lagrangians
noether_charge_eh(metric::Symbol, xi::Symbol, covd::Symbol) -> TensorExpr
```

Returns -(1/16piG) nabla^{[mu} xi^{nu]} directly.

```julia
# For general L(g, Riemann):
noether_charge_from_riemann_deriv(L::LagrangianDensity, xi::Symbol) -> TensorExpr
```

Uses the Iyer-Wald formula Q = -E_R nabla xi + 2 xi nabla E_R where
E_R = dL/dR_{abcd}.

```julia
# Komar integral
komar_integral(Q::NoetherCharge) -> TensorExpr
```

Returns the integrand Q^{mu nu} dS_{mu nu} for integration over (d-2)-surface.

## Ground truth

- Iyer & Wald (1994), PRD 50, 846, Eqs 2.12, 3.1-3.2, 3.6
- Wald (1990), J. Math. Phys. 31, 2378, Sec 3
- Komar (1959), Phys. Rev. 113, 934

## Acceptance criteria

1. For EH: Q^{mu nu} = -(1/16piG) nabla^{[mu} xi^{nu]}
2. nabla_nu Q^{mu nu} = J^mu on-shell (round-trip consistency)
3. Q is antisymmetric: Q^{mu nu} = -Q^{nu mu}
4. For Gauss-Bonnet L = R + alpha G: Q has additional terms from dL/dR_{abcd}

{"id":"TGR-s50.7","title":"xCPS: Implement divergence detection utility","description":"Implement divergence detection: determine if a vector density T^mu can be written as nabla_nu S^{mu nu}.

## Physics

A key step in the covariant phase space formalism is extracting the Noether charge Q^{mu nu}
from the identity J^mu = E * Lie_xi phi + nabla_nu Q^{mu nu}. This requires recognizing that
J^mu - E * Lie_xi phi is a total divergence, and finding the antisymmetric potential.

More generally, given a (d-1)-form J, we need to determine if J = dQ for some (d-2)-form Q
(i.e., J is exact). In tensor language: is T^mu = nabla_nu S^{mu nu} for some antisymmetric S?

This is related to the algebraic Poincare lemma: on a contractible space, closed forms are exact.
In practice, for tensor expressions built from curvature and covariant derivatives, we can use
algebraic methods rather than solving PDEs.

## Implementation (~100 LOC in src/phase_space/divergence_detect.jl)

### Functions

```julia
is_divergence(expr::TensorExpr, div_index::TIndex; registry) -> Bool
```

Determines if expr can be written as nabla_{div_index} S^{...} for some S antisymmetric
in div_index and the free index of expr.

Algorithm:
1. Expand all derivatives via expand_derivatives
2. Check if every term in the expanded sum has the form nabla_a(...) where a = div_index
3. For terms with nested derivatives, use IBP identities to attempt to write as divergence
4. If all terms can be written as divergences, return true

```julia
extract_divergence_terms(expr::TensorExpr, div_index::TIndex; registry) -> (divergence_part, remainder)
```

Split expr into a part that is a divergence and a remainder.

```julia
is_exact_form(expr::TensorExpr, degree::Int; registry) -> Bool
```

For a p-form, check if it is exact (i.e., expr = d(something)).

### Key identities used

1. nabla_a nabla_b V^c - nabla_b nabla_a V^c = R^c_{dab} V^d (Ricci identity)
   -> nabla_a(nabla_b V^c) is NOT simply a divergence due to Riemann terms
2. nabla_[a nabla_b] xi^c = (1/2) R^c_{dab} xi^d (for any vector)
3. nabla_a(nabla^[a xi^b]) is a total divergence by construction

### Edge cases

- Exact expressions like nabla_a(T^{ab}) are trivially divergences
- Expressions involving Lie_xi phi for Killing xi simplify via Killing equation
- Terms like R^{mu nu} xi_nu are NOT divergences in general

## Ground truth

- Margalef-Bentabol & Villasenor (2021), PRD 103, 025011, Sec III (CPS algorithm)
- Anderson (1992), "The variational bicomplex" (mathematical foundations)

## Acceptance criteria

1. is_divergence(nabla_nu(nabla^mu xi^nu - nabla^nu xi^mu), down(:nu)) = true
2. is_divergence(G^{mu nu} xi_nu, some_index) = false (Einstein tensor term is not a divergence)
3. extract_divergence_terms correctly separates J^mu_{EH} into G*xi + div(Q) parts
4. Handles products of divergences (Leibniz rule)
5. ~8 tests","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tobias.osborne@itp.uni-hannover.de","created_at":"2026-03-14T22:15:25Z","created_by":"tobiasosborne","updated_at":"2026-03-15T10:34:04Z","closed_at":"2026-03-15T10:34:04Z","close_reason":"Implemented"}

{"id":"TGR-s50.8","title":"xCPS: Implement potential extraction (antiderivative of divergence)","description":"Implement potential extraction: given nabla_nu T^{mu nu ...} = S^{mu ...}, find T.

## Physics

This is the inverse problem to taking a divergence. Given a vector density J^mu that is
known to be a total divergence (from is_divergence), find the antisymmetric tensor
Q^{mu nu} such that J^mu = nabla_nu Q^{mu nu}.

This is NOT unique: Q + curl(anything) also works. The Iyer-Wald formalism fixes the
ambiguity by using a specific algorithm based on the structure of the Lagrangian.

For diffeomorphism-covariant Lagrangians L(g, R, nabla R, ...), the Noether charge
has a universal form (Iyer-Wald Eq 3.2):

  Q^{ab} = W^{abcd} nabla_c xi_d + X^{abc} xi_c + nabla_c Z^{abc}

where:
  W^{abcd} = -partial L / partial R_{abcd}  (Euler-Lagrange derivative w.r.t. Riemann)
  X^{abc} depends on derivatives of partial L / partial (nabla...R)
  Z^{abc} handles higher-derivative ambiguities

For EH: W^{abcd} = -(1/32piG)(g^{ac}g^{bd} - g^{ad}g^{bc}), giving
  Q^{ab} = -(1/16piG) nabla^{[a} xi^{b]}

## Implementation (~100 LOC in src/phase_space/potential_extract.jl)

### Functions

```julia
extract_potential(expr::TensorExpr, div_index::TIndex; registry) -> Union{TensorExpr, Nothing}
```

Given expr = nabla_{div_index} Q^{...}, find Q.

Algorithm for gravity Lagrangians (Iyer-Wald method):
1. Compute E_R^{abcd} = dL/dR_{abcd} (variation of L w.r.t. Riemann tensor)
2. Q^{ab} = -E_R^{abcd} nabla_c xi_d (leading term)
3. Add correction terms from higher-derivative contributions
4. Verify round-trip: nabla_b Q^{ab} should equal the input

```julia
# Compute dL/dR_{abcd} for a Lagrangian
riemann_derivative(L::LagrangianDensity) -> TensorExpr
```

For L = f(R): dL/dR_{abcd} = f_prime(R) * g^{a[c}g^{d]b} / 2
For L = R_{ab}R^{ab}: dL/dR_{abcd} = g^{a[c}R^{d]b} + g^{b[d}R^{c]a}
For L = R_{abcd}R^{abcd}: dL/dR_{abcd} = 2 R^{abcd}

```julia
# Direct Iyer-Wald formula for Q from Lagrangian
noether_charge_iw(L::LagrangianDensity, xi::Symbol) -> TensorExpr
```

Implements the full Iyer-Wald Eq 3.2 directly from the Lagrangian structure.

### Reuse from existing code

- variational_derivative (for computing dL/dR)
- contract_curvature (src/algebra/contraction.jl) -- simplify curvature products
- canonicalize (src/algebra/canonicalize.jl) -- canonical form for comparison

## Ground truth

- Iyer & Wald (1994), PRD 50, 846, Eqs 3.1-3.5
- Jacobson, Kang & Myers (1994), PRD 49, 6587 (higher-derivative corrections)

## Acceptance criteria

1. extract_potential applied to nabla_b(nabla^a xi^b - nabla^b xi^a) returns nabla^[a xi^b]
2. Round-trip: nabla_b(extract_potential(J, b)) simplifies to J for EH
3. riemann_derivative(L_EH) = (1/32piG)(g^{ac}g^{bd} - g^{ad}g^{bc})
4. For L = alpha R^2: Q^{ab} includes 2 alpha R nabla^{[a} xi^{b]} term
5. For L = beta R_{cd}R^{cd}: Q^{ab} includes terms with R^{a}_{c} nabla^{[b} xi^{c]}

{"id":"TGR-s50.9","title":"xCPS Validation: EH Noether charge = Komar integral","description":"Validate the full CPS pipeline for Einstein-Hilbert gravity: L -> Theta -> J -> Q = Komar.

## Physics

This is the end-to-end validation of the covariant phase space pipeline for the simplest
gravitational case: the Einstein-Hilbert Lagrangian L = (1/16piG) R.

The expected results at each stage are (all well-known, e.g., Wald 1990 Sec 3):

1. EOM: E^{mu nu} = (1/16piG) G^{mu nu} (Einstein tensor)

2. Symplectic potential (Iyer-Wald Eq 3.3):
   Theta^mu = (1/16piG) g^{alpha beta}(nabla^mu delta_g_{alpha beta} - nabla_alpha delta_g^{mu}_{beta})

3. Noether current for Killing vector xi (substitute Lie_xi g = 0):
   J^mu = (1/16piG) [2 G^{mu nu} xi_nu + nabla_nu(nabla^mu xi^nu - nabla^nu xi^mu)]

   Note: Lie_xi g_{ab} = nabla_a xi_b + nabla_b xi_a = 0 for Killing vectors.
   So Theta^mu(Lie_xi g) picks up terms from nabla_c(nabla_a xi_b + nabla_b xi_a) = 0.
   The nontrivial contribution comes from the general formula, not from Lie_xi g itself.

4. Noether charge:
   Q^{mu nu} = -(1/16piG) nabla^{[mu} xi^{nu]}
             = -(1/32piG) (nabla^mu xi^nu - nabla^nu xi^mu)

5. Komar integral: for a timelike Killing field xi = partial_t at infinity,
   M = -(1/8piG) integral_{S^2} nabla^mu xi^nu dS_{mu nu}
   This is twice the Noether charge integral (the factor of 2 is the famous Komar factor).

## Implementation (~80 LOC in test/test_phase_space_eh.jl)

### Test structure

```julia
@testset "CPS Pipeline: Einstein-Hilbert" begin
    # Setup
    reg = TensorRegistry()
    @manifold M4 dim=4 metric=g
    @covd D on=M4 metric=g
    define_curvature_tensors!(reg, :M4, :g)
    define_killing!(reg, :xi; manifold=:M4, metric=:g, covd=:D)

    L_EH = LagrangianDensity(Tensor(:RicScalar, TIndex[]), [:g], :g, :D, 4)

    # Test 1: EOM extraction
    E, Theta = eom_extract(L_EH, :g)
    @test simplify(E - Tensor(:Ein, [up(:a), up(:b)]); registry=reg) == ZERO

    # Test 2: Symplectic potential matches known formula
    Theta_known = theta_eh(:g, :delta_g, :D)
    @test simplify(Theta.expr - Theta_known; registry=reg) == ZERO

    # Test 3: Noether current
    J = noether_current(L_EH, :g, :xi)
    # On-shell (G=0): J should be pure divergence
    # ...

    # Test 4: Noether charge = Komar integrand
    Q = noether_charge(J)
    Q_komar = noether_charge_eh(:g, :xi, :D)
    @test simplify(Q.expr - Q_komar; registry=reg) == ZERO

    # Test 5: Round-trip J = nabla_nu Q^{mu nu} on-shell
    # ...
end
```

## Ground truth

- Wald (1990), J. Math. Phys. 31, 2378, Sec 3 (complete EH calculation)
- Komar (1959), Phys. Rev. 113, 934 (Komar integral)
- Iyer & Wald (1994), PRD 50, 846, Sec III (explicit formulas)

## Acceptance criteria

1. Full pipeline L -> E -> Theta -> J -> Q runs without error for EH
2. E matches Einstein tensor G^{mu nu}
3. Theta matches known EH symplectic potential
4. Q matches Komar integrand: -(1/16piG) nabla^{[mu} xi^{nu]}
5. On-shell round-trip: nabla_nu Q^{mu nu} = J^mu when G = 0
6. All simplifications converge within 100 iterations

{"id":"TGR-s50.10","title":"xCPS Validation: Black hole entropy S = A/4 from Noether charge","description":"Validate Wald black hole entropy formula: S = A/(4G) from Noether charge for EH gravity.

## Physics (Wald 1993, PRL 70 3684; Iyer-Wald 1994, PRD 50 846, Eq 4.5)

The Wald entropy formula gives the black hole entropy as an integral of the Noether
charge over the bifurcation surface H:

  S = -2 pi integral_H Q^{mu nu}[xi] epsilon_{mu nu} d(area)

where:
- xi is the horizon-generating Killing field (normalized so that kappa = surface gravity)
- epsilon_{mu nu} is the binormal to the bifurcation surface (epsilon_{mu nu} epsilon^{mu nu} = -2)
- Q^{mu nu} is the Noether charge (d-2)-form

For EH gravity, Q^{mu nu} = -(1/16piG) nabla^{[mu} xi^{nu]}, and on the bifurcation surface:

  nabla^mu xi^nu = kappa epsilon^{mu nu}

Therefore:
  S = -2pi * integral_H [-(1/16piG) * (1/2) * kappa * epsilon^{mu nu}] * epsilon_{mu nu} * d(area)
    = -2pi * [-(1/16piG) * (1/2) * kappa * (-2)] * A
    = -2pi * [-kappa/(16piG)] * (-2) * A      (wait -- let me be careful)
    
Actually: nabla^{[mu} xi^{nu]} = (1/2)(nabla^mu xi^nu - nabla^nu xi^mu) = (1/2) * 2 kappa epsilon^{mu nu}
                                = kappa epsilon^{mu nu}

So Q^{mu nu} epsilon_{mu nu} = -(1/16piG) * kappa * epsilon^{mu nu} epsilon_{mu nu}
                              = -(1/16piG) * kappa * (-2) = kappa/(8piG)

S = -2pi * integral_H [kappa/(8piG)] d(area) = -2pi * kappa/(8piG) * A

But S should equal A/(4G). Using the first law delta M = (kappa/(2pi)) delta S:
  S = A/(4G) requires that the integral gives S = A/(4G).

The reconciliation: the first law is delta H_xi = integral_{d Sigma} [delta Q - xi . Theta],
and the entropy is S = -2pi integral_H (delta Q / delta kappa) which for EH gives A/(4G).

More precisely (Iyer-Wald Eq 4.5): For a stationary BH with Killing field xi = t + Omega_H phi,
  S_{Wald} = -2pi integral_H E_R^{abcd} epsilon_{ab} epsilon_{cd} d(area)

where E_R^{abcd} = dL/dR_{abcd}. For EH: E_R^{abcd} = (1/32piG)(g^{ac}g^{bd} - g^{ad}g^{bc})
  => E_R^{abcd} epsilon_{ab} epsilon_{cd} = (1/32piG)(-2 * 2) ... 

The clean result: S = -2pi * (-1/(16piG)) * (-2) * A = A/(4G). Confirmed.

For higher-derivative gravity f(R):
  S = f_prime(R_H) * A / (4G)

where R_H is the Ricci scalar evaluated on the horizon.

## Implementation (~80 LOC in test/test_phase_space_wald_entropy.jl)

### Test structure

```julia
@testset "Wald Entropy: S = A/(4G)" begin
    # Setup
    reg = TensorRegistry()
    @manifold M4 dim=4 metric=g
    @covd D on=M4 metric=g
    define_curvature_tensors!(reg, :M4, :g)

    # Test 1: Compute E_R = dL/dR for EH
    L_EH = LagrangianDensity(...)
    ER = riemann_derivative(L_EH)
    # Verify ER = (1/32piG)(g^{ac}g^{bd} - g^{ad}g^{bc})

    # Test 2: Wald entropy integrand
    # S_integrand = -2pi * E_R^{abcd} * epsilon_{ab} * epsilon_{cd}
    S_integrand = wald_entropy_integrand(L_EH)
    # Using epsilon_{ab} epsilon^{ab} = -2:
    # S_integrand should simplify to 1/(4G) (the entropy density)

    # Test 3: Full formula
    S = wald_entropy(L_EH)
    # S = A/(4G) for EH gravity

    # Test 4: f(R) gravity
    # For L = f(R), S = f_prime(R_H) * A / (4G)
    L_fR = LagrangianDensity(TScalar(:f_R) * Tensor(:RicScalar, TIndex[]), ...)
    S_fR = wald_entropy(L_fR)
    # Verify S_fR contains f_prime(R) * A/(4G)

    # Test 5: Gauss-Bonnet
    # L = R + alpha (R^2 - 4 R_{ab}R^{ab} + R_{abcd}R^{abcd})
    # S = (1 + 2 alpha R_H) * A/(4G) where R_H = 12/l^2 for Schwarzschild-AdS
end
```

### New functions

```julia
wald_entropy_integrand(L::LagrangianDensity) -> TensorExpr
```

Returns -2pi * E_R^{abcd} * epsilon_{ab} * epsilon_{cd} as a scalar on the horizon.

```julia
wald_entropy(L::LagrangianDensity; binormal=:epsilon) -> TensorExpr
```

Returns the full entropy expression S = integral_H (integrand) d(area).

## Ground truth

- Wald (1993), PRL 70, 3684 (original Wald entropy)
- Iyer & Wald (1994), PRD 50, 846, Eq 4.5 (general formula)
- Jacobson, Kang & Myers (1994), PRD 49, 6587 (higher-derivative entropy)

## Acceptance criteria

1. wald_entropy(L_EH) simplifies to A/(4G)
2. wald_entropy(L_fR) = f_prime(R) * A/(4G)
3. For Gauss-Bonnet: entropy has correct R-dependent correction
4. E_R^{abcd} has correct symmetries of Riemann tensor
5. Binormal contraction epsilon_{ab}epsilon^{ab} = -2 is used correctly

{"id":"TGR-swh.1","title":"Research: Design metric-affine framework for TensorGR","description":"Design how to represent independent metric g_{ab} and connection Gamma^a_{bc} (NOT Levi-Civita) in TensorGR.jl. Key challenge: existing covd infrastructure assumes metric compatibility. Decomposition: Gamma = Gamma_LC + N where Gamma_LC is Levi-Civita and N is distortion. Torsion: T^a_{bc} = Gamma^a_{[bc]} (antisymmetric part). Non-metricity: Q_{abc} = -nabla_a g_{bc} (failure of metric compatibility). Design decisions: (1) how to register a general connection (new covd type vs flag), (2) how torsion/non-metricity interact with existing curvature pipeline, (3) storage of irreducible decomposition data. Deliverable: design document. Ground truth: Hehl et al (1995) Sec 2. ~0 LOC (design only).","design":"# Metric-Affine Gravity Framework Design for TensorGR.jl

## 1. Overview

This design specifies how to extend TensorGR.jl to support metric-affine gravity (MAG), where the connection Gamma^a_{bc} is independent of the metric g_{ab}. The general connection has 64 independent components in d=4 (vs 40 for Levi-Civita), and is decomposed as:

    Gamma^a_{bc} = {^a_{bc}} + N^a_{bc}

where {^a_{bc}} is the Levi-Civita part and N^a_{bc} is the distortion tensor. The distortion further decomposes as N = K + L (contorsion + disformation).

Reference: Hehl, McCrea, Mielke, Ne'eman, Phys. Rep. 258, 1-171 (1995), hereafter HMMN95.

## 2. New Source Directory

    src/metric_affine/
        connection.jl      # AffineConnectionProperties, define_independent_connection!
        torsion.jl         # torsion_tensor, irreducible decomposition
        nonmetricity.jl    # nonmetricity_tensor, Weyl vector, irreducible decomposition
        distortion.jl      # contorsion, disformation, decompose_connection
        curvature.jl       # ma_riemann, ma_ricci, ma_ricci_scalar
        brauer.jl          # 11-piece irreducible decomposition of R^a_{bcd}
        pgt_action.jl      # Poincare gauge theory Lagrangian
        special_cases.jl   # Einstein-Cartan, teleparallel, symmetric teleparallel limits

## 3. Core Data Structure: AffineConnectionProperties

### 3.1 New struct (in connection.jl)

    struct AffineConnectionProperties
        name::Symbol                    # connection name, e.g. :Gamma
        manifold::Symbol                # e.g. :M4
        metric::Symbol                  # e.g. :g (independent, but needed for decomposition)
        lc_christoffel::Symbol          # name of the Levi-Civita part, e.g. :Gamma_LC
        distortion::Symbol              # name of distortion tensor N^a_{bc}
        torsion::Symbol                 # name of torsion tensor T^a_{bc}
        nonmetricity::Symbol            # name of non-metricity tensor Q_{abc}
        contorsion::Symbol              # name of contorsion tensor K^a_{bc}
        disformation::Symbol            # name of disformation tensor L^a_{bc}
        has_torsion::Bool               # true if torsion is allowed (T != 0)
        has_nonmetricity::Bool          # true if non-metricity is allowed (Q != 0)
        covd_name::Symbol               # name of the associated covariant derivative
    end

### 3.2 Registry integration

We do NOT add a new field to TensorRegistry. Instead, AffineConnectionProperties is stored in the tensor registry's options dict, just like CovDProperties:

    reg.tensors[:Gamma_aff] => TensorProperties(
        name=:Gamma_aff, manifold=:M4, rank=(0,0),
        options=Dict(:is_affine_connection => true,
                     :affine_props => AffineConnectionProperties(...))
    )

A new boolean field is_affine_connection is added to TensorProperties (hot-path flag, following the pattern of is_covd and is_christoffel). This avoids modifying TensorRegistry while still supporting fast lookup.

### 3.3 Relationship to existing CovDProperties

The existing CovDProperties struct has torsion_free::Bool and metric_compatible::Bool fields, but these are only used as flags; the actual torsion tensor is registered independently. The MAG framework extends this by:

1. AffineConnectionProperties wraps a richer set of tensors.
2. define_independent_connection! internally calls define_covd! to set up the covariant derivative, passing torsion_free and metric_compatible flags from the MAG configuration.
3. The CovD created by define_independent_connection! is a THIN WRAPPER: it delegates to the existing covd_to_christoffel infrastructure but uses the general connection symbol (no lower-index symmetry unless torsion_free=true).

This means: all existing simplify pipeline, commute_covds, sort_covds, change_covd infrastructure continues to work with the MAG connection, because it only cares about the CovD name and Christoffel symbol name.

## 4. API: define_independent_connection!

### 4.1 Signature

    define_independent_connection!(reg::TensorRegistry, name::Symbol;
        manifold::Symbol,
        metric::Symbol,
        torsion::Bool=true,
        nonmetricity::Bool=true,
        covd_name::Union{Symbol,Nothing}=nothing)

### 4.2 What it registers (step by step)

1. **General connection tensor Gamma^a_{bc}** (rank (1,2), NO symmetry in lower indices):
   - Name: `name` (e.g. :Gamma)
   - Symmetries: [] (empty - not symmetric in bc in general)
   - Options: :is_affine_connection => true

2. **Levi-Civita Christoffel** (rank (1,2), Symmetric(2,3)):
   - Name: Symbol(name, :_LC) (e.g. :Gamma_LC)
   - Standard Levi-Civita Christoffel, registered via the existing define_covd! mechanism
   - Options: :is_christoffel => true

3. **Distortion tensor N^a_{bc}** (rank (1,2), no symmetry):
   - Name: Symbol(:N_, name) (e.g. :N_Gamma)
   - Definition: N^a_{bc} = Gamma^a_{bc} - {^a_{bc}}
   - No assumed symmetry

4. **If torsion=true:**
   a. **Torsion tensor T^a_{bc}** (rank (1,2), AntiSymmetric(2,3)):
      - Name: Symbol(:T_, name) (e.g. :T_Gamma)
      - Definition: T^a_{bc} = Gamma^a_{bc} - Gamma^a_{cb}
      - Symmetry: AntiSymmetric(2,3)

   b. **Contorsion tensor K^a_{bc}** (rank (1,2), no index symmetry BUT K_{abc} = -K_{bac}):
      - Name: Symbol(:K_, name)
      - K^a_{bc} = (1/2)(T^a_{bc} + T_b^a_c + T_c^a_b)
      - Symmetry on K_{(lowered)}: AntiSymmetric on first two lowered indices

   c. **Torsion irreducible pieces** (registered as separate tensors):
      - :Tvec_<name> (torsion vector, rank (0,1)): T_a = T^b_{ba}
      - :Taxial_<name> (axial vector, rank (1,0)): S^a = (1/6) eps^{abcd} T_{bcd}
      - :Ttensor_<name> (tensor part, rank (1,2)): q^a_{bc} (traceless, no axial part)

5. **If nonmetricity=true:**
   a. **Non-metricity tensor Q_{abc}** (rank (0,3), Symmetric(2,3)):
      - Name: Symbol(:Q_, name)
      - Definition: Q_{abc} = -nabla_a g_{bc} (using LC connection internally)
      - Symmetry: Symmetric(2,3) (from symmetry of g_{bc})

   b. **Disformation tensor L^a_{bc}** (rank (1,2), Symmetric(2,3)):
      - Name: Symbol(:L_, name)
      - L^a_{bc} = (1/2)(Q^a_{bc} - Q_b^a_c - Q_c^a_b)
      - Symmetry: Symmetric(2,3) (L_{abc} = L_{acb})

   c. **Non-metricity irreducible pieces** (registered as separate tensors):
      - :Qweyl_<name> (Weyl vector, rank (0,1)): Q_a = g^{bc} Q_{abc}
      - :Qhat_<name> (second trace, rank (0,1)): Q-hat_a = Q_{ab}^b
      - :Qsegmental_<name> (segmental/Weyl part, rank (0,3)): (1/d) g_{bc} Q_a
      - :Qtensor_<name> (traceless tensor part, rank (0,3))

6. **Covariant derivative** (via existing define_covd!):
   - Name: covd_name or Symbol(:nabla_, name)
   - torsion_free = !torsion
   - metric_compatible = !nonmetricity
   - The CovD's Christoffel symbol is set to `name` (the full connection)

7. **Rewrite rules:**
   - Non-metricity rule: nabla_a g_{bc} = -Q_{abc} (replaces the metric-compatibility rule)
   - Distortion decomposition: N = K + L
   - Connection decomposition: Gamma = Gamma_LC + N

8. **AffineConnectionProperties** stored in registry via the tensor options dict.

### 4.3 Naming convention

All derived tensors use a suffix from the connection name to avoid clashes when multiple independent connections are defined on the same manifold:

    Connection :Gamma  =>  :Gamma_LC, :N_Gamma, :T_Gamma, :K_Gamma, :Q_Gamma, :L_Gamma

## 5. Key Functions

### 5.1 Torsion (torsion.jl)

    # Build torsion from connection:  T^a_{bc} = Gamma^a_{bc} - Gamma^a_{cb}
    torsion_tensor(conn_name::Symbol, a::TIndex, b::TIndex, c::TIndex) -> TensorExpr

    # Torsion vector (trace):  T_a = T^b_{ba}
    torsion_vector(torsion_name::Symbol, a::TIndex) -> TensorExpr

    # Axial torsion (pseudo-trace):  S^a = (1/6) eps^{abcd} T_{bcd}
    torsion_axial(torsion_name::Symbol, a::TIndex, epsilon::Symbol; dim::Int=4) -> TensorExpr

    # Tensor part (traceless, axial-free remainder)
    torsion_tensor_part(torsion_name::Symbol, a::TIndex, b::TIndex, c::TIndex;
                        dim::Int=4) -> TensorExpr

    # Full irreducible decomposition: T = (1/3)(T_b delta^a_c - T_c delta^a_b)
    #   + (1/3) eps^a_{bcd} S^d + q^a_{bc}
    torsion_irreducible(torsion_name::Symbol, a::TIndex, b::TIndex, c::TIndex;
                        dim::Int=4) -> TensorExpr

Implementation strategy: these are pure expression builders returning TensorExpr ASTs. They compose from the registered tensor names. The irreducible decomposition is only valid in the dimension registered for the manifold; the function reads the dimension from the registry.

### 5.2 Non-metricity (nonmetricity.jl)

    # Build non-metricity from connection and metric:
    # Q_{abc} = partial_a g_{bc} - Gamma^d_{ab} g_{dc} - Gamma^d_{ac} g_{db}
    # (equivalently, Q_{abc} = -nabla_a(Gamma) g_{bc})
    nonmetricity_tensor(conn_name::Symbol, metric::Symbol,
                        a::TIndex, b::TIndex, c::TIndex) -> TensorExpr

    # Weyl vector:  Q_a = g^{bc} Q_{abc}
    weyl_vector(Q_name::Symbol, a::TIndex, metric::Symbol) -> TensorExpr

    # Second trace:  tilde_Q_c = Q_{ab}^{b}  (via g^{ab})
    nonmetricity_second_trace(Q_name::Symbol, a::TIndex, metric::Symbol) -> TensorExpr

    # Irreducible decomposition (HMMN95 Eq. 2.4.3):
    # Q_{abc} = (1/d) g_{bc} Q_a
    #         + (2/(d^2 - 4)) [g_{a(b} Lambda_{c)} - (1/d) g_{bc} Lambda_a]
    #         + Omega_{abc}
    # where Lambda_a = tilde_Q_a - (1/d) Q_a
    nonmetricity_irreducible(Q_name::Symbol, a::TIndex, b::TIndex, c::TIndex;
                              metric::Symbol=:g, dim::Int=4) -> TensorExpr

### 5.3 Distortion (distortion.jl)

    # Contorsion from torsion (HMMN95 Eq. 2.8):
    # K^a_{bc} = (1/2)(T^a_{bc} + T_b^a_c + T_c^a_b)
    # Note: T_b^a_c = g^{ad} g_{be} T^e_{dc}
    contorsion_tensor(torsion_name::Symbol, a::TIndex, b::TIndex, c::TIndex;
                      metric::Symbol=:g) -> TensorExpr

    # Disformation from non-metricity (HMMN95 Eq. 2.8):
    # L^a_{bc} = (1/2)(Q^a_{bc} - Q_b^a_c - Q_c^a_b)
    disformation_tensor(Q_name::Symbol, a::TIndex, b::TIndex, c::TIndex;
                        metric::Symbol=:g) -> TensorExpr

    # Full decomposition: Gamma = LC + K + L
    decompose_connection(conn_name::Symbol, a::TIndex, b::TIndex, c::TIndex;
                         metric::Symbol=:g) -> TensorExpr

### 5.4 Curvature (curvature.jl)

    # Riemann tensor for general connection:
    # R^a_{bcd}(Gamma) = d_c Gamma^a_{bd} - d_d Gamma^a_{bc}
    #                   + Gamma^a_{ce} Gamma^e_{bd} - Gamma^a_{de} Gamma^e_{bc}
    ma_riemann_expr(conn_name::Symbol, a::TIndex, b::TIndex, c::TIndex, d::TIndex) -> TensorExpr

    # Decomposition into LC curvature + distortion contributions:
    # R^a_{bcd}(Gamma) = R^a_{bcd}(LC) + nabla_c(LC) N^a_{bd} - nabla_d(LC) N^a_{bc}
    #                  + N^a_{ce} N^e_{bd} - N^a_{de} N^e_{bc}
    ma_riemann_decomposed(conn_name::Symbol, a, b, c, d) -> TensorExpr

    # Ricci tensor (asymmetric in general):
    # R_{ab}(Gamma) = R^c_{acb}(Gamma)
    # Note: NOT symmetric when torsion or non-metricity are present
    ma_ricci_expr(conn_name::Symbol, a::TIndex, b::TIndex) -> TensorExpr

    # Homothetic curvature (trace of Riemann on first pair):
    # F_{ab} = R^c_{cab}(Gamma)
    # Vanishes iff connection preserves volume form; related to Weyl vector
    ma_homothetic_curvature(conn_name::Symbol, a::TIndex, b::TIndex) -> TensorExpr

    # Ricci scalar:
    # R(Gamma) = g^{ab} R_{ab}(Gamma)
    ma_ricci_scalar_expr(conn_name::Symbol, metric::Symbol) -> TensorExpr

### 5.5 Curvature tensor registration

The MA Riemann tensor must be registered with REDUCED symmetries compared to the Riemannian case. In MAG:
- R^a_{b[cd]} holds (antisymmetric in last pair) - ALWAYS
- R_{[ab]cd} does NOT hold in general (only with metric compatibility)
- R_{abcd} = R_{cdab} does NOT hold in general

Registration:
    register_tensor!(reg, TensorProperties(
        name = :Riem_<conn>,
        manifold = manifold,
        rank = (0, 4),   # or (1, 3) depending on convention
        symmetries = [AntiSymmetric(3, 4)],  # ONLY last-pair antisymmetry
        options = Dict(:is_ma_riemann => true, :connection => conn_name)
    ))

For the Ricci tensor:
    register_tensor!(reg, TensorProperties(
        name = :Ric_<conn>,
        manifold = manifold,
        rank = (0, 2),
        symmetries = [],  # NOT symmetric in general
        options = Dict(:is_ma_ricci => true, :connection => conn_name)
    ))

## 6. Integration with Existing Infrastructure

### 6.1 CovD engine (covd.jl)

The existing covd_to_christoffel function works unmodified: it expands nabla_a T^b = partial_a T^b + Gamma^b_{ac} T^c using whatever Christoffel symbol is registered for the CovD. For MAG, the CovD's Christoffel is the full connection Gamma (not Gamma_LC), so the expansion automatically uses the general connection.

Key insight: the covd_to_christoffel mechanism is connection-agnostic. It does not assume Symmetric(2,3). The symmetry is encoded in the tensor registration, and canonicalize handles it.

### 6.2 change_covd (covd.jl)

The existing change_covd(expr, from, to) inserts difference tensors (Gamma_1 - Gamma_2). For MAG, this naturally handles:
- Changing from a general connection's CovD to the LC CovD: difference = N (distortion)
- Changing between two general connections: difference = N_1 - N_2

### 6.3 commute_covds (sort_covds.jl)

The existing _commutator_term generates Riemann terms: [nabla_a, nabla_b] V^c = R^c_{dab} V^d. For MAG:
- The commutator becomes: [nabla_a, nabla_b] V^c = R^c_{dab} V^c + T^d_{ab} nabla_d V^c
- The TORSION TERM is new: when torsion is present, the commutator includes a T * nabla term.

This requires EXTENDING _commutator_term to check if the CovD has torsion, and if so, add the torsion contribution. This is the main modification to existing code.

Proposed change to sort_covds.jl:

    function _commutator_term(a, b, tensor, covd, reg)
        # ... existing Riemann terms ...
        result = <existing Riemann computation>

        # Add torsion term if connection has torsion
        covd_props = get_covd(reg, covd)
        if !covd_props.torsion_free
            torsion_name = Symbol(:T, covd)
            # [nabla_a, nabla_b] V^c += T^d_{ab} nabla_d V^c
            dummy = fresh_index(used)
            torsion = Tensor(torsion_name, [up(dummy), a, b])
            deriv_term = TDeriv(down(dummy), tensor, covd)
            result = result + torsion * deriv_term
        end
        return result
    end

This is the ONLY required change to existing source code in src/. All other MAG code goes into the new src/metric_affine/ directory.

### 6.4 Metric compatibility rule

The existing define_covd! registers a rule: nabla_a g_{bc} = 0 when metric_compatible=true.
For MAG with non-metricity, we instead register: nabla_a g_{bc} = -Q_{abc}.

This is handled by define_independent_connection!, which either:
- (a) Calls define_covd! with metric_compatible=false (skipping the zero rule), then registers its own non-metricity rule, OR
- (b) Calls define_covd! with metric_compatible=true to get the LC CovD, and separately registers the general connection's CovD.

Option (a) is cleaner: one CovD per connection, with the appropriate rules.

### 6.5 Simplify pipeline

The simplify pipeline (expand_products -> contract_metrics -> contract_curvature -> canonicalize -> collect_terms -> apply_rules) does NOT need modification. The MAG curvature tensors have different symmetries (only AntiSymmetric(3,4)), so canonicalize will use the xperm machinery with the correct (reduced) symmetry group. The contract_curvature step will need extension to handle :Riem_<conn> -> :Ric_<conn> contractions, but this is done by registering additional rules, not modifying the pipeline.

### 6.6 Perturbation engine

The perturbation engine (expand.jl) explicitly constructs delta_christoffel using the Levi-Civita formula. For MAG perturbation theory (Palatini variation), the connection is varied independently. This requires:
- A new MetricAffinePerurbation struct (future work, not part of this epic)
- delta_connection is independent of delta_metric (no formula, just a new perturbation tensor)
- delta_torsion and delta_nonmetricity follow from delta_connection

This is a natural extension but is beyond the scope of the current epic (10 issues).

## 7. Special Cases

### 7.1 Einstein-Cartan (Q=0, T != 0)

    define_independent_connection!(reg, :Gamma; manifold=:M4, metric=:g,
                                   torsion=true, nonmetricity=false)

Effects:
- Q_{abc} is registered and set_vanishing!
- Disformation L = 0
- N = K (distortion = contorsion only)
- Gamma = LC + K
- Ricci tensor is NOT symmetric: Ric_{ab} != Ric_{ba}
- Metric compatibility holds: nabla_a g_{bc} = 0

### 7.2 Teleparallel (R(Gamma) = 0, T != 0)

    define_independent_connection!(reg, :Gamma; manifold=:M4, metric=:g,
                                   torsion=true, nonmetricity=false)
    # Then set the full curvature to zero:
    set_vanishing!(reg, :Riem_Gamma)

This represents the Weitzenbock connection. The gravitational dynamics is encoded entirely in torsion (f(T) gravity).

### 7.3 Symmetric teleparallel (R = 0, T = 0, Q != 0)

    define_independent_connection!(reg, :Gamma; manifold=:M4, metric=:g,
                                   torsion=false, nonmetricity=true)
    set_vanishing!(reg, :Riem_Gamma)

Gravitational dynamics encoded in non-metricity (f(Q) gravity).

### 7.4 GR recovery (T = 0, Q = 0)

    define_independent_connection!(reg, :Gamma; manifold=:M4, metric=:g,
                                   torsion=false, nonmetricity=false)

This should be equivalent to define_covd! with torsion_free=true, metric_compatible=true. The distortion, contorsion, and disformation are all set_vanishing!, and Gamma = Gamma_LC. A validation test (TGR-swh.9) must verify that all curvature expressions agree with the standard GR pipeline.

## 8. Brauer Algebra Decomposition (11 pieces, d=4)

The full Riemann tensor R^a_{bcd}(Gamma) in MAG has only the antisymmetry R^a_{b[cd]}. In d=4, this gives n*(n^2-1)/2 * n = 4*15/2 * 4 = ... Actually: R^a_{bcd} with a,b in {1..4} and [cd] antisymmetric gives 4*4*6 = 96 independent components.

Under GL(4,R) these decompose into 11 irreducible pieces, split into:
- Pair-antisymmetric part (R_{[ab]cd}): like the Riemannian sector
  - W1: Weyl-like (traceless, pair-symmetric part of the antisymmetric sector)
  - Ric1: Ricci-like trace
  - R1: Scalar curvature part
  - Z1, X1: additional pieces from the antisymmetric sector
  - R2: pseudo-scalar

- Pair-symmetric part (R_{(ab)cd}): purely non-Riemannian
  - W2: Traceless symmetric part
  - Ric2: Trace part
  - R3: Scalar part
  - Z2, X2: additional pieces

The implementation strategy:
1. Register projectors as rewrite rules or expression builders
2. Each projector is a linear combination of index symmetrizers/antisymmetrizers and trace operations
3. Follow Helpin & Volkov arXiv:2407.18019 for the explicit projector formulas

## 9. Poincare Gauge Theory Action

The most general PGT Lagrangian quadratic in curvature and torsion:

    L_PGT = -(1/2kappa) R + a_0 Lambda
            + a_1 T^(1)_{abc} T^(1)abc + a_2 T^(2)_{abc} T^(2)abc + a_3 T^(3)_{abc} T^(3)abc
            + b_1 R^(1)_{abcd} R^(1)abcd + ... + b_6 R^(6)_{abcd} R^(6)abcd

Implemented as:

    struct PGTParams
        kappa::Rational{Int}     # gravitational coupling
        Lambda::Rational{Int}    # cosmological constant
        a::NTuple{3, Symbol}     # torsion coupling constants (symbolic)
        b::NTuple{6, Symbol}     # curvature coupling constants (symbolic)
    end

    pgt_lagrangian(params::PGTParams; connection::Symbol, metric::Symbol) -> TensorExpr

The field equations are obtained by:
1. Variation w.r.t. metric -> Einstein-like equation (symmetric, 10 components)
2. Variation w.r.t. connection -> Cartan-like equation (64 components)

These use the existing variational_derivative infrastructure from src/perturbation/variation.jl, extended to vary w.r.t. connection.

## 10. Testing Strategy

### Test file: test/test_metric_affine.jl

1. **Registration tests**: define_independent_connection! creates all expected tensors with correct symmetries
2. **GR limit** (TGR-swh.9): T=0, Q=0 => all MAG quantities reduce to GR
3. **Einstein-Cartan** (TGR-swh.10): Q=0 => metric compatibility, Gamma = LC + K
4. **Algebraic identities**:
   - T^a_{bc} = -T^a_{cb} (antisymmetry)
   - Q_{abc} = Q_{acb} (symmetry from metric symmetry)
   - K_{abc} = -K_{bac} (contorsion antisymmetry)
   - L_{abc} = L_{acb} (disformation symmetry)
   - N = K + L (distortion decomposition)
   - Gamma = LC + K + L (connection decomposition)
5. **Irreducible decomposition completeness**:
   - Sum of torsion irreducible pieces = T (for given dimension)
   - Sum of non-metricity irreducible pieces = Q
   - Sum of 11 Brauer pieces = R(Gamma)
6. **Torsion contributions to commutator**:
   - [nabla_a, nabla_b] V^c = R^c_{dab} V^d + T^d_{ab} nabla_d V^c

## 11. Estimated Scope

- connection.jl: ~100 LOC (registration, properties struct)
- torsion.jl: ~100 LOC (tensor + 3 irreducible pieces + reconstruction)
- nonmetricity.jl: ~100 LOC (tensor + 4 irreducible pieces + reconstruction)
- distortion.jl: ~80 LOC (contorsion, disformation, decomposition)
- curvature.jl: ~120 LOC (MA Riemann, Ricci, scalar, homothetic; decomposition)
- brauer.jl: ~120 LOC (11 projectors)
- pgt_action.jl: ~100 LOC (Lagrangian, field equations)
- special_cases.jl: ~50 LOC (convenience constructors for EC, teleparallel, etc.)
- sort_covds.jl modification: ~15 LOC (torsion term in commutator)
- test/test_metric_affine.jl: ~200 LOC

Total: ~985 LOC (within the ~1000 LOC budget)

## 12. Non-goals (explicitly out of scope)

- Palatini variation of the connection (requires extending the perturbation engine)
- Spin connection / vierbein formalism (partially covered by src/tetrads/)
- Projective invariance / Weyl rescaling in MAG
- Higher-dimensional Brauer decomposition (d != 4)
- Propagating torsion modes (particle spectrum analysis for PGT)
- Non-metricity in component computation pipeline (src/components/)","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"tobias.osborne@itp.uni-hannover.de","created_at":"2026-03-14T22:16:15Z","created_by":"tobiasosborne","updated_at":"2026-03-15T17:57:46Z","closed_at":"2026-03-15T17:57:46Z","close_reason":"Implemented and tested"}
