{"id":"TGR-ub6","title":"Research: Design EFT Feynman diagram infrastructure for gravitational n-point functions","description":"Design how to represent tensor n-point functions (propagators, vertices) symbolically in TensorGR.jl for the EFT of post-Newtonian gravity (Goldberger & Rothstein hep-th/0409156; Porto 1104.2712).

## Research findings

### Decision 1: Introduce new FeynmanDiagram type hierarchy (not extend KineticKernel)

KineticKernel is designed for bilinear decomposition of quadratic actions (2-point functions only). The n-point infrastructure needs:
- TensorVertex: n-point tensor structure with grouped indices (one pair per leg) and per-leg momenta
- TensorPropagator: 2-index-group object with momentum and gauge parameter
- FeynmanDiagram: graph of vertices connected by propagators, with topology, loop momenta, symmetry factors
- DiagramAmplitude: result of diagram contraction (external indices + tensor expression + loop integrals)

Rationale: the index grouping structure (pairs of Lorentz indices per graviton leg) has no analog in KineticKernel, which tracks individual bilinear terms. The graph topology (vertices, edges, loops) is fundamentally different from the flat list of bilinear terms.

### Decision 2: Momentum routing — all-incoming convention

At each n-point vertex, all momenta are incoming: k_1 + k_2 + ... + k_n = 0. For internal lines (propagators), the momentum is determined by momentum conservation at the vertices. Loop momenta are the L = E - V + 1 independent integration variables (E = edges, V = vertices). This matches the convention in Goldberger & Rothstein and is compatible with the existing to_fourier infrastructure.

### Decision 3: Index contraction via existing contract_metrics engine

The core contraction operation (propagator D^{abcd} contracted with vertex index slots) maps directly to the existing contract_metrics engine in src/algebra/contraction.jl. The key addition is:
- A "connect" operation that identifies index groups between vertices and propagators, renames indices to create dummy pairs, then calls contract_metrics
- ensure_no_dummy_clash from src/ast/indices.jl is critical for combining multiple vertices/propagators without index collisions
- fresh_index generates unique dummy names for each contraction

### Decision 4: Interface with Fourier/momentum infrastructure

- Vertices are generated via expand_perturbation (order n) followed by to_fourier — reusing the existing perturbation engine
- Momentum factors k_a in the vertex expression are Tensor(:k, [down(:a)]) — the same representation used by to_fourier
- contract_momenta (k_a k^a -> k^2) from src/action/kernel_extraction.jl handles momentum contractions
- For loop integrals: introduce MomentumIntegral type that wraps the tensor numerator and propagator denominators

### Decision 5: Gauge fixing as separate layer

The gauge-fixing action and Faddeev-Popov ghosts are separated from the pure GR vertex rules:
- gauge_fixing_action produces the gauge-fixing Lagrangian term
- ghost_propagator and ghost-graviton vertices are separate TensorPropagator/TensorVertex objects
- The gauge parameter xi flows through the propagator expression
- This allows easy switching between gauges (de Donder, Landau, background field)

## Decomposition into implementation issues

7 issues created with dependency chain:

Layer 0 (this issue - design): TGR-ub6
Layer 1 (foundations):
  TGR-ec6 — Feynman diagram type hierarchy and builder API (~120 LOC, src/feynman/types.jl)
  TGR-4ic — Gauge-fixing action and Faddeev-Popov ghost sector (~80 LOC, src/feynman/gauge_fixing.jl)
Layer 2 (physics):
  TGR-vzx — Graviton propagator in harmonic gauge (~80 LOC, src/feynman/propagator.jl)
  TGR-qks — 3-point and 4-point graviton vertices (~150 LOC, src/feynman/vertices.jl)
  TGR-9vn — Matter-graviton coupling vertices (~100 LOC, src/feynman/matter_vertices.jl)
Layer 3 (automation):
  TGR-d42 — Tensor contraction engine for diagrams (~120 LOC, src/feynman/contraction.jl)
Layer 4 (loops):
  TGR-xex — Loop integral representation with dim-reg (~100 LOC, src/feynman/loop_integrals.jl)
Downstream (not part of this design issue):
  TGR-ar7 — PN potential extraction via Fourier transform
  TGR-v8u — Validation: reproduce 1PN Einstein-Infeld-Hoffmann potential

Total: ~650 LOC in new src/feynman/ directory, plus ~200 LOC tests.

## Key references

- Goldberger & Rothstein, PRD 73 (2006) 104029, hep-th/0409156 — EFT of NRGR
- Porto, Phys. Rept. 633 (2016) 1-104, 1104.2712 — EFTofPNG review
- Sannan, PRD 34 (1986) 1749 — explicit 3-graviton vertex
- DeWitt, Phys. Rev. 162 (1967) 1195 — gravity Feynman rules
- Passarino & Veltman, Nucl. Phys. B 160 (1979) 151 — PV reduction
- Levi & Steinhoff, JHEP 06 (2015) 059, 1510.06752 — spin-gravity coupling","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"tobias.osborne@itp.uni-hannover.de","created_at":"2026-03-14T22:31:03Z","created_by":"tobiasosborne","updated_at":"2026-03-15T10:52:09Z","closed_at":"2026-03-15T10:52:09Z","close_reason":"Closed"}

{"id":"TGR-vdm.1","title":"Research: Design Dirac-Bergmann constraint analysis for TensorGR","description":"Design how to implement canonical/Hamiltonian formalism in TensorGR.jl. Key concepts: (1) Phase space: canonical pairs (q_i, p^i) where q are configuration variables and p are conjugate momenta, (2) Legendre transform: p^i = dL/d(dq_i/dt), H = p^i q_dot^i - L, (3) Poisson brackets: {f,g} = df/dq dp/dp - df/dp dg/dq, (4) Constraints: relations phi(q,p) ~ 0 arising from singular Legendre transform (degenerate Hessian), (5) Classification: first-class (generate gauge transformations) vs second-class (physical constraints). Design decisions: (1) how to represent phase space variables in TensorGR AST, (2) how Poisson brackets act on tensor expressions, (3) how to detect/classify constraints algorithmically. Deliverable: design document. Ground truth: Henneaux & Teitelboim (1992) Ch 1. ~0 LOC (design only).","design":"# Dirac-Bergmann Constraint Analysis: Design Document

## 1. Overview

This document specifies the design for a Hamiltonian analysis module in TensorGR.jl, implementing the Dirac-Bergmann algorithm for constrained systems. The module enables automated counting of propagating degrees of freedom -- the central question for any gravity theory.

Reference texts: Dirac (1964) "Lectures on Quantum Mechanics"; Henneaux & Teitelboim (1992) "Quantization of Gauge Systems" (H&T); Arnowitt, Deser, Misner (1962); Barker (2023, arXiv:2310.07484) for HiGGS.

## 2. File Layout

New directory: src/hamiltonian/

    src/hamiltonian/
      types.jl           -- HamiltonianSystem, CanonicalPair, ConstraintSet structs
      adm.jl             -- ADM decomposition: g_{ab} -> (N, N^i, gamma_{ij})  [TGR-vdm.2]
      poisson.jl         -- Poisson bracket for tensor field functionals        [TGR-vdm.3]
      constraints.jl     -- Primary/secondary constraint detection              [TGR-vdm.4/5]
      classify.jl        -- First-class vs second-class classification          [TGR-vdm.6]
      dof.jl             -- DOF counting formula + summary pipeline             [TGR-vdm.7]
      dirac_bracket.jl   -- Dirac bracket from second-class constraints         [stretch]

Test file: test/test_hamiltonian.jl (covers TGR-vdm.8 GR and TGR-vdm.9 Proca validation)

## 3. Core Type Hierarchy

### 3.1 CanonicalPair

Represents a conjugate pair (q_i, p^i) on a spatial slice.

    struct CanonicalPair
        config::Symbol         # configuration variable name (e.g. :gamma)
        momentum::Symbol       # conjugate momentum name (e.g. :pi)
        config_rank::Tuple{Int,Int}   # index structure of q (e.g. (0,2) for gamma_{ij})
        momentum_rank::Tuple{Int,Int} # index structure of p (e.g. (2,0) for pi^{ij})
        spatial_dim::Int       # dimension of spatial slice (e.g. 3)
        n_components::Int      # number of independent components (e.g. 6 for symmetric 3x3)
    end

The fundamental Poisson bracket for a canonical pair is:
  {q_{i...}(x), p^{j...}(y)} = symmetrized_delta * delta^d(x-y)

For a symmetric rank-2 pair: {gamma_{ij}(x), pi^{kl}(y)} = (1/2)(delta^k_i delta^l_j + delta^k_j delta^l_i) delta^3(x-y).
For a scalar pair: {N(x), pi_N(y)} = delta^3(x-y).
For a vector pair: {N_i(x), pi_N^j(y)} = delta^j_i delta^3(x-y).

### 3.2 ConstraintExpr

Wraps a TensorExpr with metadata about its origin and classification.

    @enum ConstraintOrigin PrimaryOrigin SecondaryOrigin TertiaryOrigin
    @enum ConstraintClass Unclassified FirstClass SecondClass

    struct ConstraintExpr
        name::Symbol               # human-readable name (e.g. :H, :H_i, :pi_N)
        expr::TensorExpr           # the constraint expression phi(q,p) ~ 0
        origin::ConstraintOrigin   # primary, secondary, ...
        class::ConstraintClass     # first-class, second-class, or unclassified
        rank::Tuple{Int,Int}       # index structure (e.g. (0,0) for scalar, (0,1) for H_i)
        n_components::Int          # number of independent constraint equations
    end

Weak equality (approx) means "zero on the constraint surface." We track this via the ConstraintClass and do not introduce a new AST node; instead, simplification on the constraint surface is achieved by passing constraint-derived RewriteRules to the simplify pipeline.

### 3.3 HamiltonianSystem

The central container, analogous to LagrangianDensity in the phase_space module.

    struct HamiltonianSystem
        # Configuration
        canonical_pairs::Vector{CanonicalPair}
        spatial_dim::Int
        manifold::Symbol

        # Hamiltonian
        hamiltonian_canonical::TensorExpr    # H_can = p q_dot - L (after Legendre)
        hamiltonian_total::TensorExpr        # H_T = H_can + lambda^alpha phi_alpha

        # Constraints (populated by the algorithm)
        primary_constraints::Vector{ConstraintExpr}
        secondary_constraints::Vector{ConstraintExpr}
        all_constraints::Vector{ConstraintExpr}   # union, maintained in discovery order

        # Classification results
        first_class::Vector{ConstraintExpr}
        second_class::Vector{ConstraintExpr}

        # Lagrange multipliers
        multipliers::Vector{Symbol}        # lambda^alpha for each primary constraint
        determined_multipliers::Dict{Symbol, TensorExpr}  # solved multipliers

        # Metadata
        registry::TensorRegistry
        lagrangian::Union{LagrangianDensity, Nothing}  # source Lagrangian if available
    end

Design decision: HamiltonianSystem is mutable. The Dirac-Bergmann algorithm populates fields incrementally (primary -> secondary -> classification). This matches the algorithmic flow and avoids reconstructing the entire struct at each step.

## 4. Algorithm Steps

### 4.1 Legendre Transform (src/hamiltonian/adm.jl for GR, generic in types.jl)

Input: LagrangianDensity L(q, q_dot), list of configuration variables and their velocity symbols.
Output: HamiltonianSystem with canonical_pairs and hamiltonian_canonical.

Algorithm:
1. Compute canonical momenta: p^i = delta L / delta q_dot_i
   - For each config variable, use variational_derivative (existing src/perturbation/variation.jl)
   - The velocity is identified by a naming convention: q_dot = Symbol(:dot_, q) or from foliation time derivative
2. Construct canonical Hamiltonian: H_can = sum_i (p^i * q_dot^i) - L
   - Build the TensorExpr for p * q_dot as a TProduct
   - Subtract the Lagrangian
3. Check Hessian regularity: W_{ij} = delta^2 L / delta q_dot^i delta q_dot^j
   - If det(W) = 0 (singular), the Legendre transform is incomplete
   - Relations between p and q that cannot be solved for q_dot are primary constraints

Integration with existing infrastructure:
- Reuse LagrangianDensity from src/phase_space/eom.jl
- Reuse variational_derivative from src/perturbation/variation.jl
- Reuse solve_tensors from src/algebra/solve.jl to attempt inversion

For GR specifically (ADM):
- Uses FoliationProperties from src/foliation/foliation.jl to identify time direction
- The ADM decomposition g_{ab} -> (N, N^i, gamma_{ij}) uses existing split_spacetime/split_all_spacetime
- Extrinsic curvature K_{ij} from src/gr/hypersurface.jl provides the velocities: dot{gamma}_{ij} = 2NK_{ij} + D_i N_j + D_j N_i

API:
    legendre_transform(L::LagrangianDensity, config_vars::Vector{Symbol},
                       velocity_vars::Vector{Symbol};
                       foliation::FoliationProperties,
                       registry::TensorRegistry=current_registry()) -> HamiltonianSystem

    adm_decompose(L::LagrangianDensity;
                   foliation::FoliationProperties,
                   registry::TensorRegistry=current_registry()) -> HamiltonianSystem

### 4.2 Primary Constraint Detection (src/hamiltonian/constraints.jl)

Input: HamiltonianSystem (after Legendre transform).
Output: HamiltonianSystem with primary_constraints populated.

Algorithm:
1. For each canonical pair, examine the momentum definition p^i = delta L / delta q_dot_i
2. Attempt to solve for q_dot_i using solve_tensors (existing solver)
3. If solve_tensors fails (linear algebra fails, or the expression is independent of q_dot): the momentum definition itself IS a primary constraint
4. Package each unsolvable relation as a ConstraintExpr with PrimaryOrigin

For GR: pi_N = delta L_EH / delta dot{N} = 0 (N has no time derivative in EH action), pi_{N^i} = 0 (shift has no time derivative). These are detected because the Lagrangian is independent of dot{N} and dot{N^i}.

API:
    find_primary_constraints!(H::HamiltonianSystem;
                               registry::TensorRegistry=current_registry()) -> Vector{ConstraintExpr}

Mutates H.primary_constraints in place and returns the list.

### 4.3 Total Hamiltonian Construction

After primary constraints are found:
    H_T = H_can + sum_alpha lambda^alpha * phi_alpha

where lambda^alpha are Lagrange multiplier symbols. Register multipliers as new tensors in the registry with appropriate rank matching the constraint's complementary index structure.

### 4.4 Secondary Constraint Generation (src/hamiltonian/constraints.jl)

Input: HamiltonianSystem with primary_constraints and hamiltonian_total.
Output: HamiltonianSystem with secondary_constraints populated.

Dirac-Bergmann consistency algorithm:
    repeat:
        for each undetermined constraint phi:
            compute phi_dot = {phi, H_T}
            simplify phi_dot on constraint surface (apply constraint rules)
            if phi_dot == 0:
                identity, skip (consistency satisfied trivially)
            elif phi_dot is independent of all lambda^alpha:
                new constraint chi(q,p) ~ 0  ->  add to secondary_constraints
            elif phi_dot depends on lambda^alpha:
                solve for lambda^alpha  ->  store in determined_multipliers
            end
        end
    until no new constraints generated

Implementation details:
- The Poisson bracket {phi, H_T} is computed using poisson_bracket (see 4.5)
- "Independent of lambda" is checked by _contains_unknown(expr, Set(multiplier_names))
- "Simplify on constraint surface" uses RewriteRules generated from constraints: make_rule(phi.expr, ZERO) for each phi
- Termination: the phase space is finite-dimensional, so at most 2N constraints can arise (where N = phase space dim)
- Safety: hard limit of maxiter=100 iterations (should terminate in ~3 for GR)

For GR: consistency of pi_N ~ 0 gives {pi_N, H_T} = H ~ 0 (Hamiltonian constraint). Consistency of pi_{N^i} ~ 0 gives {pi_{N^i}, H_T} = H_i ~ 0 (momentum constraints). Consistency of H, H_i with H_T gives conditions that determine the multipliers (no tertiary constraints).

API:
    generate_secondary_constraints!(H::HamiltonianSystem;
                                     maxiter::Int=100,
                                     registry::TensorRegistry=current_registry()) -> Vector{ConstraintExpr}

    find_constraints!(H::HamiltonianSystem; ...) -> HamiltonianSystem
    # Convenience: calls find_primary_constraints! then generate_secondary_constraints!

### 4.5 Poisson Bracket (src/hamiltonian/poisson.jl)

The Poisson bracket for field-theory functionals on a spatial slice.

For canonical pairs (q_I, p^I) with I indexing all pairs:
    {F, G} = sum_I integral d^d x [ (delta F / delta q_I) (delta G / delta p^I) - (delta F / delta p^I) (delta G / delta q_I) ]

Implementation strategy:
- For density expressions (not smeared/integrated), the Poisson bracket produces delta-function terms
- The functional derivative delta F / delta q is computed via variational_derivative (existing)
- The integral over the spatial slice and delta-function contraction is handled algebraically:
  the result is the contraction of functional derivatives with symmetrized deltas

For practical constraint analysis, we work with SMEARED constraints:
    phi[N] = integral d^3 x  N(x) phi(x)  (smeared with test function N)

The Poisson bracket of smeared constraints is again a smeared quantity, and the delta functions integrate out cleanly.

Alternatively, following HiGGS/Hamilcar, we can work at the level of constraint DENSITIES and track the delta-function structure algebraically. For TensorGR, the density approach is more natural since our expressions are already tensor densities.

Key design decision: We implement poisson_bracket for local density expressions. The output is a TensorExpr representing the Poisson bracket density. Delta functions from fundamental brackets integrate out when both arguments are local functions of canonical variables.

For constraint densities phi(q, p) and chi(q, p) that depend only on fields and their spatial derivatives at a single point, the Poisson bracket reduces to:
    {phi, chi} = sum_I [ (partial phi / partial q_I) (partial chi / partial p^I) - (partial phi / partial p^I) (partial chi / partial q_I) ]
              + derivative terms from IBP when phi/chi contain spatial derivatives of q, p.

This is exactly the structure of variational_derivative, applied to a finite-dimensional (componentwise) setting.

API:
    poisson_bracket(F::TensorExpr, G::TensorExpr,
                    pairs::Vector{CanonicalPair};
                    registry::TensorRegistry=current_registry()) -> TensorExpr

    # Convenience for HamiltonianSystem
    poisson_bracket(F::TensorExpr, G::TensorExpr,
                    H::HamiltonianSystem) -> TensorExpr

### 4.6 Constraint Classification (src/hamiltonian/classify.jl)

Input: HamiltonianSystem with all_constraints fully populated.
Output: HamiltonianSystem with first_class and second_class populated.

Algorithm (H&T Sec 1.3-1.4):
1. Compute the constraint matrix C_{alpha beta} = {phi_alpha, phi_beta} for all constraint pairs
2. Evaluate on the constraint surface (apply constraint RewriteRules)
3. Find the null space of C:
   - Null vectors correspond to first-class constraints
   - The rank of C gives the number of second-class constraints
4. Reclassify: constraints in the null space are first-class; the rest are second-class

For the matrix operations: use sym_det and related functions from src/action/quadratic_action.jl for symbolic matrix rank/null-space computation.

Important subtlety: constraint matrix entries are TensorExpr, not numbers. "Null space" means finding linear combinations of constraints whose brackets with ALL other constraints vanish weakly (on the constraint surface). This is a symbolic computation. For known theories (GR, Proca), we verify against expected results rather than implementing general symbolic null-space computation.

For GR: all 8 constraints (4 primary + 4 secondary) are first-class. The constraint algebra is:
    {H[N], H[M]} = H_i[gamma^{ij}(N d_j M - M d_j N)]   (structure functions, not constants)
    {H_i[N^i], H[M]} = H[N^i d_i M]
    {H_i[N^i], H_j[M^j]} = H_i[N^j d_j M^i - M^j d_j N^i]

For Proca: pi^0 ~ 0 and (d_i pi^i + m^2 A^0) ~ 0 have bracket = m^2 delta^3 (nonzero for m != 0), so both are second-class.

API:
    classify_constraints!(H::HamiltonianSystem;
                          registry::TensorRegistry=current_registry()) -> (Vector{ConstraintExpr}, Vector{ConstraintExpr})
    # Returns (first_class, second_class) and mutates H

### 4.7 DOF Counting (src/hamiltonian/dof.jl)

Formula (H&T Eq 1.47):
    n_DOF = n_pairs - n_first_class - (1/2) * n_second_class

where:
- n_pairs = sum of n_components for each CanonicalPair (total phase space dim / 2)
- n_first_class = sum of n_components for each first-class ConstraintExpr
- n_second_class = sum of n_components for each second-class ConstraintExpr

The formula counts configuration-space DOF (physical propagating modes). Phase-space DOF = 2 * n_DOF.

    struct DOFCount
        n_config_vars::Int       # total configuration variables
        n_phase_space::Int       # = 2 * n_config_vars
        n_first_class::Int       # first-class constraint components
        n_second_class::Int      # second-class constraint components (must be even)
        n_dof::Int               # propagating DOF = n_config_vars - n_first_class - n_second_class/2
    end

Validation:
- GR: 10 pairs (1 N + 3 N^i + 6 gamma_{ij}) - 8 first_class - 0 second_class = 2 DOF
- Proca: 4 pairs (A_0, A_i) - 0 first_class - 1 second_class_pair = 3 DOF
- Maxwell: 4 pairs - 2 first_class (pi^0, Gauss) - 0 second_class = 2 DOF

API:
    count_dof(H::HamiltonianSystem) -> DOFCount

    dof_summary(L::LagrangianDensity; foliation, variables, ...) -> DOFCount
    # End-to-end: Legendre -> constraints -> classify -> count

## 5. Integration with Existing Modules

### 5.1 Phase Space Module (src/phase_space/)

The covariant phase space formalism (Iyer-Wald) works with the full spacetime. The Hamiltonian module works with the 3+1 split. Connection points:
- LagrangianDensity is the input to both
- The symplectic form omega from symplectic.jl is related to the Poisson bracket via inversion: {F, G} = omega^{-1}(delta F, delta G)
- noether_current and HamiltonianVariation from first_law.jl provide the covariant form of the Hamiltonian; the ADM Hamiltonian is its 3+1 decomposition

### 5.2 Foliation Module (src/foliation/)

The ADM decomposition reuses:
- FoliationProperties for identifying temporal/spatial directions
- split_spacetime / split_all_spacetime for decomposing expressions
- svt_constraint_rules as a pattern for implementing constraint RewriteRules

### 5.3 Perturbation Module (src/perturbation/)

- variational_derivative provides functional differentiation
- MetricPerturbation provides linearization infrastructure
- The Hessian W_{ij} = d^2L/d(q_dot^i)(q_dot^j) is computed via double application of variational_derivative

### 5.4 Algebra Module (src/algebra/)

- solve_tensors for inverting momentum definitions (detecting primary constraints)
- simplify pipeline for constraint-surface simplification
- RewriteRules for encoding constraint equations as simplification rules

### 5.5 Hypersurface Module (src/gr/hypersurface.jl)

- extrinsic_curvature_expr provides K_{ij}
- SubmanifoldProperties provides the geometric setup for the spatial slice

## 6. Constraint Surface Simplification

A key technical requirement: simplification "on the constraint surface" (weak equality).

Implementation: for each constraint phi_alpha ~ 0, generate a RewriteRule that sets phi_alpha -> 0. These rules are passed to the simplify pipeline via:
    simplify(expr; registry=reg, extra_rules=constraint_rules)

This requires a minor extension to the simplify API to accept additional rules beyond those in the registry. Alternatively, the constraint rules can be registered directly in the TensorRegistry via register_rule!.

Design decision: Use a dedicated ConstraintRuleSet that wraps Vector{RewriteRule} and can be passed to simplify. This keeps constraint rules separate from the global rule set and allows easy composition.

## 7. Worked Example: GR

Step-by-step for the validation (TGR-vdm.8):

1. Start with EH Lagrangian: L_EH = sqrt(gamma) * N * (K_{ij}K^{ij} - K^2 + R^{(3)})
   where K_{ij} = (1/2N)(dot{gamma}_{ij} - D_i N_j - D_j N_i)

2. Configuration variables: N (scalar), N^i (vector, 3 components), gamma_{ij} (symmetric tensor, 6 components). Total: 10.

3. Canonical momenta:
   - pi_N = delta L / delta dot{N} = 0  (L independent of dot{N})  ->  PRIMARY CONSTRAINT
   - pi_{N^i} = delta L / delta dot{N^i} = 0  (L independent of dot{N^i})  ->  PRIMARY CONSTRAINT
   - pi^{ij} = delta L / delta dot{gamma}_{ij} = sqrt(gamma)(K^{ij} - gamma^{ij}K)

4. Canonical Hamiltonian: H_can = pi^{ij} dot{gamma}_{ij} - L = N*H + N^i*H_i + boundary terms
   where H = (pi^{ij}pi_{ij} - (1/2)pi^2)/sqrt{gamma} - sqrt{gamma}*R^{(3)}
   and H_i = -2 D_j pi^j_i

5. Total Hamiltonian: H_T = H_can + lambda*pi_N + lambda^i*pi_{N^i}

6. Secondary constraints:
   - {pi_N, H_T} = delta H_can / delta N = H ~ 0  ->  SECONDARY (Hamiltonian constraint)
   - {pi_{N^i}, H_T} = delta H_can / delta N^i = H_i ~ 0  ->  SECONDARY (momentum constraints)

7. Tertiary check: {H, H_T} ~ 0 and {H_i, H_T} ~ 0 determine the multipliers lambda, lambda^i.
   No new constraints arise.

8. Classification: all 8 constraints are first-class (they generate spacetime diffeomorphisms).

9. DOF: 10 pairs - 8 first_class - 0 second_class = 2. Matches: two graviton polarizations.

## 8. Worked Example: Proca

For the validation (TGR-vdm.9):

1. Proca Lagrangian: L = -(1/4)F_{ab}F^{ab} + (1/2)m^2 A_a A^a
   3+1 split: L = (1/2)(E_i E^i) - (1/4)F_{ij}F^{ij} + (1/2)m^2(A_i A^i - A_0^2)
   where E^i = dot{A}^i - d^i A_0 = F^{0i}

2. Configuration variables: A_0 (scalar, 1), A_i (vector, 3). Total: 4.

3. Canonical momenta:
   - pi^0 = delta L / delta dot{A_0} = 0  ->  PRIMARY CONSTRAINT
   - pi^i = delta L / delta dot{A_i} = E^i = F^{0i}  (invertible)

4. H_can = (1/2)pi^i pi_i + (1/4)F_{ij}F^{ij} - (1/2)m^2 A_i A^i + A_0(d_i pi^i) + (1/2)m^2 A_0^2

5. H_T = H_can + lambda * pi^0

6. Secondary: {pi^0, H_T} = d_i pi^i + m^2 A_0 ~ 0  ->  SECONDARY (modified Gauss law)
   Tertiary check: {d_i pi^i + m^2 A_0, H_T} determines lambda. No new constraints.

7. Classification: {pi^0, d_i pi^i + m^2 A_0} = m^2 delta^3(x-y) != 0.
   Both constraints are second-class (2 components total).

8. DOF: 4 pairs - 0 first_class - (1/2)(2 second_class) = 3. Matches: massive spin-1 has 3 polarizations.

## 9. Stretch Goals

### 9.1 Dirac Bracket

For second-class constraints chi_alpha, the Dirac bracket is:
    {f, g}_D = {f, g} - {f, chi_alpha} C^{alpha beta} {chi_beta, g}

where C^{alpha beta} = ({chi_alpha, chi_beta})^{-1}.

This requires inverting the constraint bracket matrix symbolically. For simple cases (e.g., Proca with scalar constraints), C is a scalar and inversion is trivial. For more complex cases, we need symbolic matrix inversion from src/action/quadratic_action.jl.

    dirac_bracket(f::TensorExpr, g::TensorExpr,
                  second_class::Vector{ConstraintExpr},
                  pairs::Vector{CanonicalPair};
                  registry=current_registry()) -> TensorExpr

### 9.2 Gauge Generator

For first-class constraints, construct the gauge generator:
    G = epsilon^alpha phi_alpha  (with gauge parameter epsilon)

The gauge transformation of any phase-space function F is:
    delta_gauge F = {F, G}

### 9.3 Higher-Derivative Theories

For Lagrangians with higher time derivatives (e.g., R^2 gravity), the Ostrogradsky method introduces additional canonical pairs for each derivative order. This requires extending the Legendre transform to handle L(q, dot{q}, ddot{q}).

## 10. Error Handling

- legendre_transform errors if velocity variables are not found in the Lagrangian
- find_primary_constraints! logs warnings for over-determined systems (more constraints than expected)
- generate_secondary_constraints! errors if maxiter is exceeded (possible sign of implementation bug)
- classify_constraints! warns if n_second_class is odd (inconsistent, signals an error)
- count_dof validates: n_dof >= 0, n_second_class is even

## 11. Testing Strategy

test/test_hamiltonian.jl should cover:
1. Unit tests for each function in isolation (Poisson bracket algebra, constraint detection, etc.)
2. GR validation (TGR-vdm.8): full pipeline, verify 2 DOF
3. Proca validation (TGR-vdm.9): full pipeline, verify 3 DOF
4. Maxwell validation: Proca with m=0, verify 2 DOF (gauge symmetry restored)
5. Scalar field: L = (1/2)(d phi)^2 - V(phi), verify 1 DOF (no constraints)
6. Regression: constraint algebra closure for GR (structure functions)

## 12. Estimated Size

Following the epic estimate of ~900 LOC total:
- types.jl: ~80 LOC
- adm.jl: ~120 LOC  (TGR-vdm.2)
- poisson.jl: ~120 LOC  (TGR-vdm.3)
- constraints.jl: ~200 LOC  (TGR-vdm.4 + TGR-vdm.5)
- classify.jl: ~120 LOC  (TGR-vdm.6)
- dof.jl: ~80 LOC  (TGR-vdm.7)
- test/test_hamiltonian.jl: ~200 LOC  (TGR-vdm.8 + TGR-vdm.9)

## 13. Key Design Decisions Summary

D1. HamiltonianSystem is MUTABLE -- algorithm fills fields incrementally.
D2. Constraints are TensorExpr with metadata, not a new AST node -- reuses existing simplify pipeline.
D3. Poisson bracket operates on local density expressions -- delta functions are implicit.
D4. Constraint-surface simplification via RewriteRules -- reuses existing rule engine.
D5. ADM decomposition reuses FoliationProperties -- no parallel infrastructure.
D6. solve_tensors detects primary constraints via failed inversion -- reuses existing solver.
D7. Classification via constraint bracket matrix -- symbolic rank/null-space for known theories.
D8. n_components on CanonicalPair and ConstraintExpr -- enables DOF counting without expanding indices.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"tobias.osborne@itp.uni-hannover.de","created_at":"2026-03-14T22:19:18Z","created_by":"tobiasosborne","updated_at":"2026-03-15T17:57:46Z","closed_at":"2026-03-15T17:57:46Z","close_reason":"Implemented and tested"}

{"id":"TGR-vzx","title":"EFTofPNG: Implement graviton propagator in harmonic gauge","description":"Implement the graviton propagator in de Donder (harmonic) gauge as a symbolic TensorExpr.

## Background

In the EFT approach to post-Newtonian gravity (Goldberger & Rothstein, hep-th/0409156, Sec 2; Porto, 1104.2712, Sec 2.1), the graviton propagator is the Green's function of the linearized Einstein equations in harmonic gauge. Working with the metric decomposition g_{ab} = eta_{ab} + kappa * h_{ab} (where kappa = sqrt(32 pi G)), the de Donder gauge condition partial^b h_bar_{ab} = 0 (h_bar = h - (1/2) eta h) gives:

    D^{abcd}(k) = (eta^{ac} eta^{bd} + eta^{ad} eta^{bc} - eta^{ab} eta^{cd}) / (2 k^2)

This propagator has the symmetries D^{abcd} = D^{bacd} = D^{abdc} = D^{cdab}.

## Integration with existing infrastructure

Must build on:
- `KineticKernel` and `spin_project` from `src/action/kernel_extraction.jl` — the propagator is the inverse of the spin-2 + spin-0 kinetic operator
- `theta_projector`, `omega_projector` from `src/action/spin_projectors.jl` — decomposition into spin sectors
- `contract_momenta` from `src/action/kernel_extraction.jl` — contraction k_a k^a -> k^2
- `Tensor`, `TProduct`, `TScalar`, `up`, `down` from `src/types.jl`
- `to_fourier` from `src/svt/fourier.jl` — momentum space representation

## Function signatures

```julia
# Primary constructor
graviton_propagator(a::TIndex, b::TIndex, c::TIndex, d::TIndex;
                    metric::Symbol=:eta, k_sq::Symbol=:k2,
                    dim::Int=4) -> TensorExpr

# Convenience: propagator with momentum dependence
graviton_propagator_full(a::TIndex, b::TIndex, c::TIndex, d::TIndex,
                         k::Symbol=:k) -> TensorExpr

# Gauge parameter family: R_xi gauge
graviton_propagator_Rxi(a, b, c, d; xi=1, ...) -> TensorExpr
# xi=1 is de Donder (harmonic), xi=0 is Landau gauge
```

## Ground truth (verification tests)

1. **Trace test**: eta_{ac} eta_{bd} D^{abcd}(k) = (d^2 - 3d + 2) / (2 k^2) = 3/k^2 in d=4.
   Reference: standard textbook result; Sannan PRD 34 (1986) 1749.

2. **Spin-2 projection**: P^(2)_{abcd} D^{cdef} = (1/k^2) P^(2)_{abef}.
   The propagator projects purely onto spin-2 + spin-0s sectors (no spin-1).

3. **Inverse relation**: D^{abcd} K_{cdef}^{EH} = (1/2)(delta^a_e delta^b_f + delta^a_f delta^b_e)
   where K^{EH} is the Einstein-Hilbert kinetic kernel from extract_kernel_direct.

4. **Pole structure in NRGR**: For non-relativistic GR (|k0| << |k|), the propagator reduces to the instantaneous potential propagator D^{0000} -> 1/(2 k^2), D^{00ij} -> -delta^{ij}/(2 k^2). Reference: Goldberger & Rothstein hep-th/0409156 Eq. 4.

## Implementation notes

- Store as TensorExpr using existing AST (not a new type)
- Use TScalar for 1/k^2 factor via _sym_div
- Register the propagator tensor with symmetries Symmetric(1,2), Symmetric(3,4), PairSymmetric((1,2),(3,4))
- ~80 LOC in src/feynman/propagator.jl","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tobias.osborne@itp.uni-hannover.de","created_at":"2026-03-14T22:31:12Z","created_by":"tobiasosborne","updated_at":"2026-03-15T17:44:28Z","closed_at":"2026-03-15T17:44:28Z","close_reason":"Implemented and merged"}

{"id":"TGR-wbm","title":"BH-Pert2: Implement mode coupling coefficient tables for (l1,m1)x(l2,m2)->(l,m)","description":"Implement precomputed mode coupling coefficient tables that store the angular coupling coefficients C^{lm}_{l1 m1, l2 m2} for second-order BH perturbation theory. At second order, every bilinear product of first-order modes (l1,m1) and (l2,m2) sources modes with l in [|l1-l2|, l1+l2] and m = m1+m2. The coupling coefficients encode the angular integral information.

Structure:
- `ModeCouplingTable` struct: stores precomputed C^{lm}_{l1 m1, l2 m2} for all valid (l1, l2, l, m1, m2) up to a given lmax
- `build_coupling_table(lmax; parity=:all)` -> `ModeCouplingTable`
  Iterates over l1 in 0:lmax, l2 in 0:lmax, computes allowed l3, calls angular_integral functions
- `coupling_coefficient(table, l1, m1, l2, m2, l, m)` -> Float64
- Separate tables for even-even, odd-odd, and even-odd couplings (6 parity combinations)
- Selection rules enforced: m = m1 + m2; |l1-l2| <= l <= l1+l2; parity conservation (l1+l2+l must be even for scalar Gaunt)

The table is a Dict{NTuple{6,Int}, Float64} keyed by (l1,m1,l2,m2,l,m) -> C. For lmax=10 this is O(10^4) entries, fast to compute and store.

Must depend on the angular integration engine (TGR-bw9) for the raw integrals.

File: src/perturbation/mode_coupling.jl (~100 LOC)
Reference: Brizuela, Martin-Garcia & Tiglio, PRD 80, 024021 (2009), Appendix A, Table I; Gleiser et al, Phys. Rep. 325, 41 (2000), Sec 4.2.
Test: (1) C^{0,0}_{l,0,l,0} = (-1)^l sqrt((2l+1)/(4pi)) (orthonormality, Edmonds Eq 4.6.3). (2) Sum rule: sum_{l3} |C^{l3,m3}_{l1,m1,l2,m2}|^2 = (2l1+1)(2l2+1)/(4pi). (3) Table at lmax=4 reproduces Brizuela et al Table I values.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tobias.osborne@itp.uni-hannover.de","created_at":"2026-03-15T10:21:09Z","created_by":"tobiasosborne","updated_at":"2026-03-15T16:30:22Z","closed_at":"2026-03-15T16:30:22Z","close_reason":"Implemented and merged"}

{"id":"TGR-wd6","title":"Constraints Validation: Weyl tensor trace-free and Bianchi identities auto-simplify","description":"End-to-end validation of the constraint enforcement system using the Weyl tensor. Setup: register Weyl C_{abcd} as (1) trace-free on all index pairs: C^a_{bad} = 0, (2) first Bianchi identity: C_{a[bcd]} = 0, (3) divergence-free in vacuum: nabla^a C_{abcd} = 0. Verify: (a) g^{ac} C_{abcd} automatically simplifies to zero (trace-free), (b) C_{abcd} + C_{acdb} + C_{adbc} automatically simplifies to zero (cyclic/Bianchi), (c) in vacuum spacetime, nabla^a C_{abcd} simplifies to zero (divergence-free). Ground truth: Wald, General Relativity (1984), Eq 3.2.28, 3.2.14; standard Weyl tensor identities. Test: STRING MATCH — all three expressions simplify to TScalar(0), confirming automatic constraint enforcement.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"tobias.osborne@itp.uni-hannover.de","created_at":"2026-03-14T22:34:12Z","created_by":"tobiasosborne","updated_at":"2026-03-15T08:24:03Z","closed_at":"2026-03-15T08:24:03Z","close_reason":"Implemented and merged"}

{"id":"TGR-wgy","title":"Design: Frame index VBundle for tetrad/Lorentz indices","description":"DESIGN DECISION: Tetrad/frame indices must live in a separate VBundle from coordinate (Tangent) indices.

Current state: TIndex has vbundle::Symbol (default :Tangent). BasisProperties has basis_type::Symbol (:coordinate, :tetrad, :orthonormal). VBundles are registered via define_vbundle!.

Design:
1. Register a :Lorentz VBundle (dim=d) for frame indices I,J,K,... separate from :Tangent for coordinate indices mu,nu,...
2. For null (NP) tetrads, the same :Lorentz VBundle works -- the null vs orthonormal distinction is in the frame metric eta_{IJ}, not the index type.
3. The tetrad e^a_I is a mixed tensor: one :Tangent Up index, one :Lorentz Down index.
4. Frame metric eta_{IJ} has two :Lorentz indices; spacetime metric g_{mu nu} has two :Tangent indices.
5. Canonicalization via xperm already handles multi-VBundle setups -- indices in different VBundles don't contract.

Convention (following xCoba): use lowercase Latin a,b,c for :Lorentz frame indices and Greek mu,nu,rho for :Tangent coordinate indices. In code: up(:a, :Lorentz) vs up(:mu) or up(:mu, :Tangent).

This unifies orthonormal and null tetrads: both use :Lorentz VBundle, distinguished by frame_metric being diag(-1,1,1,1) vs the NP null metric.

References: xCoba documentation (DefVBundle for frame indices); Chandrasekhar Ch 1 (index conventions); Stephani et al. Ch 3 (frame formalism).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"tobias.osborne@itp.uni-hannover.de","created_at":"2026-03-15T16:20:02Z","created_by":"tobiasosborne","updated_at":"2026-03-15T16:52:45Z","closed_at":"2026-03-15T16:52:45Z","close_reason":"Implemented, 51 tests, coexistence with spinor bundles verified"}

{"id":"TGR-wq0.1","title":"Research: Design bimetric framework for TensorGR","description":"Design how to support two dynamical metrics g_{ab} and f_{ab} on the same manifold in TensorGR.jl. Key challenge: existing TensorGR registry supports one 'active' metric per manifold for contraction/simplification. Need multi-metric support. Design decisions: (1) how to register two metrics without conflict in metric_cache (separate namespaces?), (2) separate curvature tensors Riem_g, Riem_f, Ric_g, Ric_f etc., (3) how the matrix square root S = sqrt(g^{-1}f) is represented abstractly, (4) interaction with existing simplify pipeline (which metric contracts indices?). Note: existing product_manifold.jl already handles multi-metric in different factor spaces -- bimetric needs SAME manifold. Deliverable: design document. Ground truth: Hassan & Rosen (2012); Torsello et al (2019). ~0 LOC (design only).","design":"# Bimetric Gravity Framework Design for TensorGR.jl

## 1. Problem Statement

Hassan-Rosen bimetric gravity has two dynamical metrics g_{ab} and f_{ab} on the SAME
manifold M, interacting through a non-derivative potential built from the matrix
square root S = sqrt(g^{-1}f). The core challenge for TensorGR.jl is that the current
registry's metric_cache maps each manifold to a SINGLE metric, and the contraction
engine uses this mapping to decide which metric raises/lowers indices. Bimetric gravity
needs two metrics on the same manifold, each with its own curvature hierarchy and
covariant derivative, plus a non-standard matrix-valued tensor S whose abstract
representation requires careful design.

References: Hassan & Rosen, JHEP 02 (2012) 126 [arXiv:1109.3515];
de Rham, Gabadadze & Tolley (2011) [arXiv:1011.1232];
Torsello, Kocic & Mortsell (2019) [arXiv:1904.10464] (bimEX).

## 2. Registry Strategy: VBundle-Keyed Dual Metrics

### The Existing Pattern

The spinor metric (src/spinors/spin_metric.jl) and frame bundle (src/tetrads/frame_bundle.jl)
already solved the same fundamental problem: multiple metrics on the same manifold. Their
solution is to key metric_cache by VBUNDLE rather than manifold:

    reg.metric_cache[:SL2C] = :eps_spin
    reg.metric_cache[:Lorentz] = :eta

Both save/restore the manifold-level cache entry so register_tensor! does not clobber
the spacetime metric.

### Bimetric Solution

Define two vector bundles on the same manifold, one for each metric sector:

    :TangentG  -- the "g-sector" tangent bundle, indices from [:a,:b,:c,:d,:e,:f]
    :TangentF  -- the "f-sector" tangent bundle, indices from [:i,:j,:k,:l,:m,:n]

Then:
    reg.metric_cache[:TangentG] = :g
    reg.metric_cache[:TangentF] = :f

The contraction engine already resolves metrics via _find_metric_for_vbundle(reg, vbundle, manifold),
which checks metric_cache[vbundle] first, falling back to metric_cache[manifold]. So g-sector
indices (vbundle=:TangentG) contract with :g, and f-sector indices (vbundle=:TangentF) contract
with :f. No changes to the contraction engine are needed.

The manifold-level metric_cache[:M4] stores whichever metric is designated "primary" (typically :g).
This is the metric used for index raising/lowering on tensors with vbundle=:Tangent (the default).

### Implementation in define_bimetric!

```julia
struct BimetricProperties
    manifold::Symbol
    g_metric::Symbol          # name of first metric
    f_metric::Symbol          # name of second metric
    g_vbundle::Symbol         # :TangentG
    f_vbundle::Symbol         # :TangentF
    g_covd::Symbol            # Levi-Civita for g
    f_covd::Symbol            # Levi-Civita for f
    sqrtmat::Symbol           # name of square-root matrix tensor S
    betas::Vector{Symbol}     # [beta0, beta1, beta2, beta3, beta4]
    dim::Int
end

function define_bimetric!(reg::TensorRegistry;
                          manifold::Symbol=:M4,
                          g::Symbol=:g, f::Symbol=:f,
                          g_indices::Vector{Symbol}=[:a,:b,:c,:d,:e,:f],
                          f_indices::Vector{Symbol}=[:i,:j,:k,:l,:m,:n],
                          betas::Vector{Symbol}=[:beta0,:beta1,:beta2,:beta3,:beta4])
    mp = get_manifold(reg, manifold)
    d = mp.dim

    # Register g-sector vbundle and metric
    define_vbundle!(reg, :TangentG; manifold=manifold, dim=d, indices=g_indices)
    # ... register g as metric on :TangentG ...
    # Save/restore manifold metric_cache (spinor pattern)

    # Register f-sector vbundle and metric
    define_vbundle!(reg, :TangentF; manifold=manifold, dim=d, indices=f_indices)
    # ... register f as metric on :TangentF ...

    # Register sector-specific curvature tensors (product_manifold pattern):
    #   Riem_g, Ric_g, RicScalar_g, Ein_g, Weyl_g
    #   Riem_f, Ric_f, RicScalar_f, Ein_f, Weyl_f
    _register_sector_curvature!(reg, manifold, g, :TangentG)
    _register_sector_curvature!(reg, manifold, f, :TangentF)

    # Register sector-specific covariant derivatives
    g_covd = Symbol(:nabla_, g)
    f_covd = Symbol(:nabla_, f)
    define_covd!(reg, g_covd; manifold=manifold, metric=g)
    define_covd!(reg, f_covd; manifold=manifold, metric=f)

    # Register square-root matrix S and its properties
    # Register beta parameters as scalar constants
    # Store BimetricProperties in reg.foliations[:bimetric_M4]

    bp = BimetricProperties(manifold, g, f, :TangentG, :TangentF,
                            g_covd, f_covd, :S_gf, betas, d)
    reg.foliations[Symbol(:bimetric_, manifold)] = bp
    bp
end
```

The pattern of using reg.foliations as a generic metadata store follows existing practice
(ProductManifoldProperties, FoliationProperties are both stored there).

## 3. Square Root Matrix S = sqrt(g^{-1}f)

### Abstract Representation

The square-root matrix S^a_b satisfies S^a_c S^c_b = g^{ac} f_{cb}. It is a mixed-index
(1,1) tensor. At the abstract level, S is represented as a standard Tensor:

    register_tensor!(reg, TensorProperties(
        name=:S_gf, manifold=manifold, rank=(1,1),
        symmetries=SymmetrySpec[],
        options=Dict{Symbol,Any}(
            :is_sqrt_matrix => true,
            :g_metric => g, :f_metric => f)))

Key properties:
- S^a_b is NOT symmetric in general (it is symmetric only on proportional backgrounds)
- S^a_c S^c_b = g^{ac} f_{cb}  (defining relation -- registered as rewrite rule)
- g_{ac} S^c_b = f_{ac} (S^{-1})^c_b  (metric compatibility)

### Component Computation

At the component level (src/components/), the square root of g^{-1}f can be computed
numerically via matrix square root (LinearAlgebra.sqrt). This does not require new AST
types -- CTensor already stores component arrays.

### Trace Identities

The elementary symmetric polynomials of S need trace(S^n), which at the abstract level
is S^a_a for n=1, S^a_b S^b_a for n=2, etc. These are standard TensorExpr contractions.

## 4. Elementary Symmetric Polynomials e_n(S)

The Hassan-Rosen interaction potential uses the elementary symmetric polynomials of S:

    e_0(S) = 1
    e_1(S) = [S]                           = tr(S)
    e_2(S) = (1/2)([S]^2 - [S^2])
    e_3(S) = (1/6)([S]^3 - 3[S][S^2] + 2[S^3])
    e_4(S) = det(S)     (in d=4)

where [S^n] = tr(S^n) = S^{a1}_{a2} S^{a2}_{a3} ... S^{an}_{a1}.

### Abstract Tensor Representation

Each e_n is a scalar, represented as TScalar with a symbolic label or as an explicit
contraction expression:

```julia
function elementary_symmetric_polynomial(n::Int, S::Symbol;
                                          registry=current_registry())
    # Returns TensorExpr for e_n(S)
    # e_0 -> TScalar(1//1)
    # e_1 -> Tensor(S, [up(:a), down(:a)])  (trace contraction)
    # e_2 -> (1//2) * (e_1^2 - tr(S^2))
    # etc.
end
```

For e_2 in explicit tensor form:
    e_2(S) = (1/2)(S^a_a S^b_b - S^a_b S^b_a)

This is a standard TProduct/TSum expression with dummy contractions. No new AST types
needed. The simplify pipeline handles contraction, and canonicalization handles the
index ordering.

### Newton's Identities

The e_n can be computed recursively via Newton's identities from the power sums
p_k = tr(S^k). This is useful for both symbolic and numerical evaluation:

    e_1 = p_1
    2*e_2 = e_1*p_1 - p_2
    3*e_3 = e_2*p_1 - e_1*p_2 + p_3
    4*e_4 = e_3*p_1 - e_2*p_2 + e_1*p_3 - p_4

Implementation: a function power_trace(S, n) that builds the TensorExpr for tr(S^n)
using fresh dummy indices.

## 5. Bimetric Interaction Potential

The Hassan-Rosen potential is:

    V(g,f) = M_g^2 * sqrt(det(g)) * sum_{n=0}^{4} beta_n * e_n(S)

where S = sqrt(g^{-1}f) and beta_n are 5 free coupling constants.

### API

```julia
function bimetric_potential(bp::BimetricProperties;
                            registry=current_registry()) -> TensorExpr
    # Returns: sqrt_det_g * sum(beta_n * e_n(S), n=0:d)
    # Each term is a scalar TensorExpr
end
```

The sqrt(det(g)) factor is represented as TScalar(:sqrt_neg_det_g), consistent with
the existing metric_det_expr / sqrt_det_expr infrastructure in src/gr/metric.jl.

## 6. Bimetric Field Equations

The equations of motion are obtained by varying the total action
S = S_EH[g] + S_EH[f] + S_int[g,f] with respect to each metric.

### g-sector equation

    G^(g)_{ab} + M_g^{-2} * V_{ab}^{(g)} = 0

where V_{ab}^{(g)} = sum_{n=0}^{3} (-1)^n * beta_n * g_{ac} * Y^c_b(n, S)

and Y^c_b(n, S) are defined recursively:
    Y_0(S) = I
    Y_1(S) = S - [S]*I
    Y_2(S) = S^2 - [S]*S + (1/2)([S]^2 - [S^2])*I
    Y_3(S) = S^3 - [S]*S^2 + (1/2)([S]^2 - [S^2])*S - (1/6)([S]^3 - 3[S][S^2] + 2[S^3])*I

### f-sector equation

    G^(f)_{ab} + (M_g/M_f)^2 * sum_{n=0}^{3} (-1)^n * beta_{4-n} * f_{ac} * Y^c_b(n, S^{-1}) = 0

### API

```julia
function bimetric_eom_g(bp::BimetricProperties;
                        registry=current_registry()) -> TensorExpr
    # Returns the g-sector equation of motion (LHS = 0)
    # G^(g)_{ab} + sum of Y-tensor terms
end

function bimetric_eom_f(bp::BimetricProperties;
                        registry=current_registry()) -> TensorExpr
    # Returns the f-sector equation of motion (LHS = 0)
end
```

The Y-tensors are built as explicit TensorExpr contractions of S^a_b, computed
recursively. They are rank-(1,1) tensor expressions.

### Implementation: Y-tensor Construction

```julia
function Y_tensor(n::Int, S::Symbol, dim::Int;
                  registry=current_registry()) -> TensorExpr
    # Y_0 = delta^a_b
    # Y_n = S * Y_{n-1} - e_n * delta^a_b  (Cayley-Hamilton recursion)
    # Returns rank-(1,1) TensorExpr
end
```

## 7. Mass Eigenstates on Proportional Backgrounds

### Proportional Background

The most physically relevant background is f_0 = c^2 * g_0 for some constant c.
On this background:
- S_0 = c * delta^a_b
- The two metrics share the same Levi-Civita connection

### Mass Matrix Diagonalization

Linearize: g = g_0 + delta_g, f = c^2 * g_0 + delta_f.

Define mass eigenstates:
    chi_{ab} = delta_g_{ab} - (1/c) * delta_f_{ab}    (massless mode)
    psi_{ab} = delta_g_{ab} + c * delta_f_{ab}         (massive mode)

The massless mode has the standard linearized GR kinetic term (Lichnerowicz operator).
The massive mode acquires a Fierz-Pauli mass term:

    L_mass = -(1/2) * m_FP^2 * (psi_{ab} psi^{ab} - psi^2)

where m_FP^2 depends on the beta_n and c.

### API

```julia
struct MassEigenstates
    massless::Symbol          # tensor name for massless mode
    massive::Symbol           # tensor name for massive mode
    mass_squared::TensorExpr  # Fierz-Pauli mass squared
    mixing_angle::Any         # c parameter
end

function mass_eigenstates(bp::BimetricProperties;
                          background::Symbol=:proportional,
                          c::Symbol=:c_prop,
                          registry=current_registry()) -> MassEigenstates
    # 1. Register background proportionality constant c
    # 2. Compute S_0 = c * delta
    # 3. Compute beta-dependent mass
    #    m_FP^2 = (beta_1/c + 2*beta_2 + beta_3*c) * c * M_g^2
    # 4. Define perturbation tensors for massless/massive modes
    # 5. Return MassEigenstates
end
```

### Integration with Perturbation Engine

The mass eigenstates hook into the existing perturbation engine (src/perturbation/)
through define_metric_perturbation!:

    mp_g = define_metric_perturbation!(reg, :g, :delta_g)
    mp_f = define_metric_perturbation!(reg, :f, :delta_f)

The diagonalization is a linear transformation that maps (delta_g, delta_f) to
(chi, psi). This is implemented as rewrite rules:

    delta_g = (c*chi + psi) / (1 + c^2)
    delta_f = (-chi/c + psi) / (1 + c^2)

## 8. Fierz-Pauli Structure and Ghost Freedom

### Fierz-Pauli Mass Term

The massive mode psi_{ab} must have exactly the Fierz-Pauli structure:

    L_FP = -(m^2/2) * (psi_{ab} psi^{ab} - psi * psi)

where psi = g_0^{ab} psi_{ab} is the trace. This relative coefficient (-1) between
the tensor-squared and trace-squared terms is the unique ghost-free combination
(Boulware-Deser ghost absent).

This can be verified using the existing spin projector infrastructure:
- Extract the quadratic kernel for psi
- Project onto spin-2, spin-1, spin-0s, spin-0w sectors
- Verify: spin-2 has propagating DOF, spin-0s has the FP relative coefficient,
  spin-1 and spin-0w are non-propagating

### Degree-of-Freedom Count

Total: 7 = 5 (massive spin-2) + 2 (massless spin-2).
This is the defining property of ghost-free bimetric theory.

Validation test: form the quadratic Lagrangian for (chi, psi), extract the 2x2
kernel matrix, compute its determinant, and verify the number of propagating modes
matches 7.

## 9. Integration with Existing Infrastructure

### Components Used As-Is (No Modifications)

- **TensorExpr AST**: S, e_n, Y_n are all standard tensor expressions
- **Contraction engine**: VBundle-keyed metric lookup handles dual metrics
- **Simplify pipeline**: expand_products, contract_metrics, canonicalize all work
- **Perturbation engine**: define_metric_perturbation! for each metric
- **Spin projectors**: Barnes-Rivers for massive mode analysis
- **Quadratic action**: QuadraticForm for the (chi, psi) system
- **Component computation**: CTensor for numerical verification

### Components Extended

- **Registry**: Store BimetricProperties in reg.foliations (existing pattern)
- **Curvature**: Sector-specific names Riem_g, Riem_f (product_manifold pattern)
- **VBundles**: Two tangent bundles on same manifold (spinor/frame pattern)
- **metric_cache**: VBundle-keyed entries for dual metric resolution

### New Code (src/bimetric/)

Estimated ~600 LOC across 4 files:

    src/bimetric/
        bimetric.jl           # BimetricProperties, define_bimetric! (~100 LOC)
        potential.jl          # e_n, Y_n, bimetric_potential (~150 LOC)
        field_equations.jl    # bimetric_eom_g, bimetric_eom_f (~150 LOC)
        mass_eigenstates.jl   # mass_eigenstates, FP structure (~200 LOC)

### Test Plan

    test/test_bimetric.jl:
    1. define_bimetric! registers all tensors correctly
    2. Dual metric_cache entries populated
    3. g-sector and f-sector contractions independent
    4. e_n(c*I) = binom(d,n)*c^n for proportional S
    5. Y_n(c*I) verified against explicit formula
    6. EOM symmetry: g-equation and f-equation related by (g<->f, beta_n<->beta_{4-n})
    7. Fierz-Pauli structure of massive mode on proportional background
    8. 7 DOF count (ghost freedom)

## 10. Full API Summary

```julia
# Setup
define_bimetric!(reg; g=:g, f=:f, manifold=:M4,
                 betas=[:beta0,:beta1,:beta2,:beta3,:beta4])

# Square root matrix
sqrt_matrix(bp)  # returns Tensor(:S_gf, [up(:a), down(:b)])

# Elementary symmetric polynomials
elementary_symmetric_polynomial(n, :S_gf)  # e_n(S) as TensorExpr
power_trace(S, n)                          # tr(S^n) as TensorExpr

# Y-tensors (equation-of-motion building blocks)
Y_tensor(n, :S_gf, dim)  # Y_n(S)^a_b as TensorExpr

# Interaction potential
bimetric_potential(bp)  # V(g,f) as scalar TensorExpr

# Field equations
bimetric_eom_g(bp)  # G^(g)_{ab} + V^(g)_{ab} = 0
bimetric_eom_f(bp)  # G^(f)_{ab} + V^(f)_{ab} = 0

# Mass spectrum (proportional background)
mass_eigenstates(bp; background=:proportional, c=:c_prop)

# Fierz-Pauli verification
fierz_pauli_check(bp, me::MassEigenstates)  # -> Bool (7 DOF)
```

## 11. Key Design Decisions

1. **VBundle separation (not manifold duplication)**: Two vbundles on the same manifold,
   not two copies of the manifold. This keeps the topology unified and follows the
   established spinor/frame pattern.

2. **No new AST types**: S, e_n, Y_n are all represented as standard TensorExpr trees.
   The square-root matrix is a registered Tensor with special options, not a new subtype.

3. **Frozen metrics**: During construction of the interaction potential, the "other"
   metric must be frozen (freeze_metric!) to prevent the contraction engine from
   contracting across sectors. After potential construction, both are unfrozen.

4. **Sector curvature naming**: Follow product_manifold's pattern of metric-suffixed
   curvature names (Riem_g, Riem_f, etc.) rather than introducing a new naming scheme.

5. **Proportional background as primary**: The mass eigenstate computation assumes a
   proportional background f_0 = c^2 * g_0 as the physically most important case.
   General backgrounds (e.g., non-proportional) are deferred.

6. **Component-level square root**: The abstract S tensor has a defining algebraic
   relation (S^2 = g^{-1}f) encoded as a rewrite rule. Numerical evaluation uses
   LinearAlgebra.sqrt on the component matrix g^{-1}f. No symbolic matrix square root
   is attempted.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"tobias.osborne@itp.uni-hannover.de","created_at":"2026-03-14T22:18:04Z","created_by":"tobiasosborne","updated_at":"2026-03-15T17:44:29Z","closed_at":"2026-03-15T17:44:29Z","close_reason":"Implemented and merged"}

- xAct/Spinors uses OverDot[] for display of primed indices
- Wald Ch 13 uses primed notation: A', B'

{"id":"TGR-xb3","title":"BH-Pert2: Implement first-order Regge-Wheeler and Zerilli master equations and gauge-invariant variables","description":"Implement the first-order Regge-Wheeler (odd) and Zerilli (even) master equations as symbolic radial ODEs, together with the gauge-invariant master variables constructed from the Regge-Wheeler-Zerilli metric coefficients.

Odd parity (Regge-Wheeler):
- Gauge-invariant master function: psi^{RW}_{lm} = (f/r) [h_2 - r d(h_0/f)/dr + ... ]
  (Cunningham-Price-Moncrief variable, Martel & Poisson Eq 5.19)
- Master equation: [-d^2/dt^2 + d^2/dr*^2 - V_RW(r)] psi^{RW} = 0
  where V_RW = f [l(l+1)/r^2 - 6M/r^3]
  and r* = r + 2M ln(r/(2M) - 1) is the tortoise coordinate

Even parity (Zerilli):
- Gauge-invariant master function: psi^{Z}_{lm} = ... (Zerilli-Moncrief variable, Martel & Poisson Eq 5.12)
  Constructed from H_0, H_2, K, G and their derivatives
- Master equation: [-d^2/dt^2 + d^2/dr*^2 - V_Z(r)] psi^{Z} = 0
  where V_Z = f/(r^2 Lambda_r^2) [2 lambda^2(lambda+1)r^3 + 6 lambda^2 M r^2 + 18 lambda M^2 r + 18 M^3]
  with lambda = (l-1)(l+2)/2, Lambda_r = lambda r + 3M

Infrastructure needed:
- `RWPotential(l, M)` -> symbolic expression for V_RW(r)
- `ZerilliPotential(l, M)` -> symbolic expression for V_Z(r)
- `rw_master_variable(h0, h2, l, M)` -> symbolic expression for Cunningham-Price-Moncrief
- `zerilli_master_variable(H0, H2, K, G, l, M)` -> symbolic expression for Zerilli-Moncrief
- `MasterEquation` struct: potential V(r), source S(r) (zero at first order), master function psi
- Must support both symbolic (TensorExpr) and Symbolics.jl numeric evaluation

This enables: (a) the benchmark 05 test_skip items for Zerilli/RW master equations, (b) first-order solutions as input for the second-order source assembly.

File: src/perturbation/master_equations.jl (~150 LOC)
Reference: Regge & Wheeler, PR 108, 1063 (1957); Zerilli, PRD 2, 2141 (1970); Martel & Poisson, PRD 71, 104003 (2005), Sec V; Cunningham, Price & Moncrief, ApJ 224, 643 (1978).
Test: (1) V_RW at l=2 evaluates correctly at r=6M (ISCO). (2) V_Z at l=2 matches Zerilli 1970 Eq 12. (3) Both potentials -> 0 as r -> infinity. (4) Master variables are gauge-invariant under first-order gauge transformations (verified symbolically using gauge.jl).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tobias.osborne@itp.uni-hannover.de","created_at":"2026-03-15T10:21:54Z","created_by":"tobiasosborne","updated_at":"2026-03-15T10:59:38Z","closed_at":"2026-03-15T10:59:38Z","close_reason":"Implemented with MP Eqs 4.26/5.15, ISCO values verified, 90 tests"}

{"id":"TGR-xmm.1","title":"Research: Design index-free notation","description":"Design index-free tensor notation for TensorGR.jl. Options: (1) overload Julia dot operator (LinearAlgebra.dot or custom) for contractions like Ric . Ric meaning R_{ab}R^{ab}, (2) create IndexFree wrapper type that stores contraction pattern without explicit index names, (3) operator overloading with * for tensor product and | for contraction. Key design questions: (a) how to represent partial contractions (some indices contracted, some free), (b) how to handle multiple possible contraction patterns (R_{abcd}R^{abcd} vs R_{abcd}R^{acbd}), (c) syntax for traces, (d) conversion to/from indexed form. Ground truth: Nutma Sec 5. Output: design notes, not code.","design":"# Index-Free Notation Design for TensorGR.jl

## Decision: Translation Layer, Not Parallel AST

Index-free notation should be a **translation layer on top of TensorExpr**, not a parallel AST. Rationale:

1. **The entire simplify pipeline (xperm, contraction, canonicalization, rules) operates on TensorExpr.** Building a parallel pipeline would duplicate ~2000 lines of battle-tested code. xTras took the same approach: IndexFree is a wrapper/head, and FromIndexFree converts to indexed form before any computation.

2. **Index-free form is lossy for partial contractions.** R_{abcd}R^{abcd} vs R_{abcd}R^{acbd} are different indexed expressions but need extra annotation in index-free form. The indexed AST is the canonical computational representation.

3. **Index-free notation is primarily useful for (a) compact input, (b) clean display of fully-contracted scalars, and (c) differential-geometric operations (musical isomorphisms, directional derivatives).** All three are well-served by a translation layer.

## Architecture

### New file: `src/notation/index_free.jl` (~200 lines)

Three components:
- **IndexFreeExpr wrapper struct** — lightweight annotation on TensorExpr
- **to_index_free(expr)** — strip indices from fully-contracted expressions
- **from_index_free(expr)** — auto-generate dummy indices from registry metadata

### IndexFreeExpr struct

```julia
struct IndexFreeExpr
    expr::TensorExpr         # the underlying indexed expression
    display_form::Symbol     # :scalar, :tensor, :operator
end
```

This is NOT a subtype of TensorExpr. It is a display/input wrapper. All computation (simplify, canonicalize, contract) operates on the .expr field. This follows the xTras pattern exactly: IndexFree[expr] is a head that wraps an expression.

Why not a TensorExpr subtype: Every function in the simplify pipeline, AST walk, contraction engine, canonicalization, etc. dispatches on TensorExpr subtypes. Adding a new subtype would require updating 40+ methods. A wrapper avoids this entirely.

### to_index_free(expr) -> IndexFreeExpr

Conversion from indexed to index-free form. Algorithm:

1. Compute free_indices(expr). If non-empty, this is a tensor with free indices — keep it indexed, wrap as IndexFreeExpr with display_form=:tensor.

2. If fully contracted (no free indices): walk the AST and strip all indices from Tensor nodes, strip derivative indices from TDeriv nodes. Store the stripped display in display_form=:scalar.

3. For TDeriv: CovD name is preserved (nabla vs partial), index is dropped.

4. For TProduct: factors become juxtaposition (implied tensor product with full contraction).

The display_form field controls how show(io, ::IndexFreeExpr) renders:
- :scalar → "Ric . Ric" or "Riem * Riem" (using dot for full contraction)
- :tensor → falls back to indexed display
- :operator → for derivative operators

This follows xTras ToIndexFree: strip indices from tensors, strip the single index from CovDs, preserve nesting structure.

### from_index_free(ifexpr) -> TensorExpr

Conversion from index-free back to indexed form. Algorithm:

1. Walk the stored expression tree.
2. For each bare Tensor(name, []) (no indices): look up rank in registry, generate fresh dummy indices using SlotsOfTensor-equivalent (rank tuple). Assign Up indices for contravariant slots, Down for covariant.
3. For TDeriv with no index: generate a fresh Down index.
4. Contract repeated factors: when two tensors appear in a TProduct, pair up all indices as dummies (full contraction). Use fresh_index() to avoid clashes.
5. For partial contractions: not supported in pure index-free input — user must use the indexed API or the slot-specification API (see below).

This follows xTras FromIndexFree: FromIndexFreeTensors inserts DummyIn indices into each slot, FromIndexFreeCovDs inserts a DummyIn index into each CovD.

### Slot-Specification API for Partial Contractions

For R(X,Y)Z = R^a_{bcd} X^b Y^c Z^d, we need slot-based contraction:

```julia
contract(R, X, Y, Z)    # R(X,Y,Z) — contract R's 3 covariant slots with vectors
contract(R, :_, X, Y, Z)  # R(_,X,Y,Z) — skip first slot, contract remaining
```

Implementation: `contract(T, args...)` looks up the rank of T in the registry, iterates over args. For each non-placeholder arg, generates a fresh dummy index, assigns it to the corresponding slot of T and as the index of the arg tensor. Returns a TProduct with the appropriate contractions.

The placeholder :_ (or nothing) leaves that slot as a free index. This is the standard slot notation from differential geometry textbooks (Wald Ch. 2).

### Musical Isomorphisms: sharp/flat

```julia
sharp(omega, metric) -> TensorExpr   # raise: g^{ab} omega_b
flat(X, metric) -> TensorExpr        # lower: g_{ab} X^b
```

Implementation: these are thin wrappers around metric insertion.

- sharp(omega, g): omega must be rank-(0,1). Insert g^{ab} and contract: g^{ab} omega_b. Returns a rank-(1,0) expression.
- flat(X, g): X must be rank-(1,0). Insert g_{ab} and contract: g_{ab} X^b. Returns a rank-(0,1) expression.

These are equivalent to separate_metric() but with nicer semantics. They use the registry to look up the metric, generate fresh indices, and return the contracted product.

For higher-rank tensors: sharp(T, g, slot) and flat(T, g, slot) raise/lower a specific slot.

### inner (Pairing)

```julia
inner(omega, X) -> TensorExpr    # <omega, X> = omega_a X^a
inner(T, S, g) -> TensorExpr     # T_{ab} S^{ab} = full contraction via g
```

Implementation:
- For (1-form, vector): pair the single indices as dummies.
- For general (T, S, g): insert metrics to match all index positions, then contract all as dummies. This is the "dot product" of two tensors of the same type.

### Directional Covariant Derivative: nabla

```julia
nabla(X, Y, covd) -> TensorExpr    # nabla_X Y = X^a nabla_a Y^b
nabla(Y, covd) -> TensorExpr       # full nabla: nabla_a Y^b (returns TDeriv)
```

Implementation:
- nabla(X, Y, covd): generate fresh index a, construct X^a * TDeriv(down(a), Y, covd). The result is a rank-(1,0) expression (direction contracted away).
- nabla(Y, covd): just TDeriv(down(a), Y, covd) with fresh a. This is the "full" covariant derivative.

This parallels the existing lie_derivative(v, expr) API exactly: it takes a vector field and an expression, generates dummy indices internally.

### Integration with Existing Operations

The existing exterior calculus operations (wedge, interior_product, exterior_d, hodge_dual, cartan_lie_d) already work in a semi-index-free style — they take Tensor objects and generate indices internally. The index-free layer unifies them under a cleaner API:

```julia
# These already exist and work index-free internally:
wedge(alpha, beta, p, q)
interior_product(v, alpha)
exterior_d(alpha, degree, deriv_idx)
lie_derivative(v, expr)
lie_bracket(v, w)
```

The index-free layer adds:
- Display support: show wedge products as alpha /\ beta
- Input support: from_index_free understands wedge/interior notation
- No need to specify degree explicitly (looked up from registry)

### Display

```julia
Base.show(io::IO, ifx::IndexFreeExpr)
```

For scalar invariants (all contracted): "Ric . Ric", "nabla Riem . nabla Riem", "R^2"

to_unicode(ifx::IndexFreeExpr) and to_latex(ifx::IndexFreeExpr) support the same.

The key insight from xTras: display strips tensor indices and CovD indices, but preserves the algebraic structure (products, sums, powers, derivative nesting).

### Scope Boundaries

What index-free notation does NOT need to handle (out of scope for TGR-xmm.1):

1. **Abstract tensor algebra in index-free form.** The simplify pipeline always works on TensorExpr. You always convert from_index_free before computing.

2. **Ambiguous contractions.** If R*R has multiple possible contraction patterns, from_index_free picks the canonical one (all-contracted, first slot of each tensor paired in order). For non-canonical contractions, use the indexed API.

3. **Parallel simplification AST.** No need to duplicate canonicalize, contract_metrics, etc.

4. **Index-free rules/patterns.** Pattern matching stays in indexed form.

## API Summary

```julia
# Core types
IndexFreeExpr(expr, display_form)

# Conversion
to_index_free(expr::TensorExpr) -> IndexFreeExpr
from_index_free(ifx::IndexFreeExpr) -> TensorExpr

# Slot contraction
contract(T::Tensor, args...) -> TensorExpr

# Musical isomorphisms
sharp(omega, metric) -> TensorExpr
flat(X, metric) -> TensorExpr

# Pairing
inner(omega, X) -> TensorExpr
inner(T, S, metric) -> TensorExpr

# Directional derivative
nabla(X, Y, covd) -> TensorExpr
nabla(Y, covd) -> TensorExpr

# Display
Base.show(io, ifx::IndexFreeExpr)
to_latex(ifx::IndexFreeExpr)
to_unicode(ifx::IndexFreeExpr)
```

## File Layout

```
src/notation/
  index_free.jl      # IndexFreeExpr, to_index_free, from_index_free, contract
                      # sharp, flat, inner, nabla, display methods
```

Single file, ~200 lines. Depends on: types.jl, registry.jl, ast/indices.jl, show.jl.

## Key Design References

- xTras (Nutma 2014, Sec 5): IndexFree/ToIndexFree/FromIndexFree — wrapper-based translation layer, not a parallel AST. Our design follows this exactly.
- Wald (1984) Ch 2: Abstract index notation is the computational substrate; component-free notation is a convenience layer. Our design reflects this hierarchy.
- Penrose & Rindler (1984): Abstract indices are "placeholders for slots," not component labels. Index-free notation suppresses these placeholders when they carry no information (fully contracted scalars).","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"tobias.osborne@itp.uni-hannover.de","created_at":"2026-03-14T22:12:31Z","created_by":"tobiasosborne","updated_at":"2026-03-15T17:44:29Z","closed_at":"2026-03-15T17:44:29Z","close_reason":"Implemented and merged"}

{"id":"TGR-oun","title":"Spinors: Register SL2C and SL2C_dot VBundles with conjugation metadata","description":"## Summary

Register two new VBundles on the spacetime manifold for spinor indices:
- :SL2C (undotted/unprimed spinor bundle, dim=2, indices [:A,:B,:C,:D,:E,:F])
- :SL2C_dot (dotted/primed spinor bundle, dim=2, indices [:Ap,:Bp,:Cp,:Dp,:Ep,:Fp])

Add a :conjugate_bundle field to VBundleProperties.options linking :SL2C <-> :SL2C_dot.
This tells the contraction and canonicalization engines that these two bundles are complex
conjugates: a tensor with indices from both bundles is a mixed spinor.

## Design Rationale (from TGR-7im research)

Three options were evaluated for encoding dotted/undotted distinction:

1. **Extend IndexPosition** (SpinorUp/SpinorDown/DottedUp/DottedDown): Breaks all
   existing code that switches on Up/Down. Every function touching IndexPosition must
   be updated. Rejected.

2. **New SpinorIndex type**: Requires parallel code paths everywhere (indices(),
   free_indices(), rename_dummy(), canonicalize()). Massive code duplication. Rejected.

3. **VBundle approach** (CHOSEN): TIndex already carries vbundle::Symbol. Create
   :SL2C and :SL2C_dot bundles. The existing infrastructure already:
   - Groups indices by (name, vbundle) in _analyze_indices()
   - Checks vbundle match in contraction engine (contraction.jl line 136, 207)
   - Handles non-Tangent vbundles (define_vbundle! already exists)
   No core type changes needed. Backward compatible.

The conjugation relationship is stored as metadata on VBundleProperties via an
options dict or new field, NOT by encoding it in the index name (primes cause
ParseError in Julia).

## Functions

```julia
# In src/spinors/bundles.jl (~60 LOC)

define_spinor_bundles!(reg::TensorRegistry; manifold::Symbol=:M4)
```

Registers:
1. VBundle :SL2C on manifold, dim=2, indices=[:A,:B,:C,:D,:E,:F]
2. VBundle :SL2C_dot on manifold, dim=2, indices=[:Ap,:Bp,:Cp,:Dp,:Ep,:Fp]
3. Sets conjugation metadata: reg.vbundles[:SL2C].options[:conjugate] = :SL2C_dot
   (requires adding options::Dict{Symbol,Any} to VBundleProperties, or using
   a registry-level conjugation cache Dict{Symbol,Symbol})

## Convenience constructors

```julia
spin_up(s::Symbol) = TIndex(s, Up, :SL2C)
spin_down(s::Symbol) = TIndex(s, Down, :SL2C)
spin_dot_up(s::Symbol) = TIndex(s, Up, :SL2C_dot)
spin_dot_down(s::Symbol) = TIndex(s, Down, :SL2C_dot)
```

Up/Down still means contravariant/covariant (raised/lowered by spin metric).
The dotted/undotted distinction is carried by the vbundle, not by position.

## VBundleProperties change

Add options dict to VBundleProperties (currently has: name, manifold, dim, indices).
Options: {:conjugate => :SL2C_dot, :is_spinor => true}. This is a BREAKING change
to VBundleProperties struct -- use kwarg constructor with defaults for backward compat.

## Ground truth

- Penrose & Rindler, "Spinors and Space-Time" Vol 1 (1984), Section 2.5
- Wald, "General Relativity" (1984), Chapter 13
- xAct/Spinors package uses VBundle approach with conjugation flag

## Tests

1. define_spinor_bundles! registers both bundles on manifold
2. spin_up(:A).vbundle == :SL2C
3. spin_dot_down(:Ap).vbundle == :SL2C_dot
4. Conjugation metadata round-trip: conjugate of :SL2C is :SL2C_dot and vice versa
5. TIndex(:A, Up, :SL2C) != TIndex(:A, Up, :SL2C_dot) (different vbundles)
6. Backward compat: existing VBundleProperties(...) 4-arg constructor still works","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tobias.osborne@itp.uni-hannover.de","created_at":"2026-03-15T10:20:17Z","created_by":"tobiasosborne","updated_at":"2026-03-18T20:01:11Z"}

{"id":"TGR-e1l","title":"Spinors: Implement spinor dummy pair analysis for mixed-bundle expressions","description":"## Summary

Extend _analyze_indices() in src/ast/indices.jl so that spinor dummy pairs are
correctly identified. Currently, a dummy pair requires matching (name, vbundle)
with one Up and one Down occurrence. This already works for spinor indices
because TIndex carries vbundle -- an :SL2C index will never accidentally pair
with an :SL2C_dot index or a :Tangent index.

However, fresh_index() must be extended to generate spinor-appropriate fresh
names. Currently it cycles through :a-:z. For :SL2C it should cycle through
:A-:F (from the VBundle's indices list), and for :SL2C_dot through :Ap-:Fp.
Also, rename_dummy() and rename_dummies() need no changes (they operate on
Symbol names, vbundle-agnostic), but _normalize_dummies_for_display() needs
spinor-aware canonical dummy name lists.

## Functions to modify

```julia
# src/ast/indices.jl

# Extend fresh_index to accept vbundle hint:
fresh_index(used::Set{Symbol}; vbundle::Symbol=:Tangent) -> Symbol
# For :SL2C, cycles [:A,:B,:C,:D,:E,:F,:A1,:B1,...]
# For :SL2C_dot, cycles [:Ap,:Bp,:Cp,:Dp,:Ep,:Fp,:Ap1,:Bp1,...]
# For :Tangent (default), existing behavior

# Extend _normalize_dummies_for_display for spinor dummies:
# Canonical spinor dummy names: [:P,:Q,:R,:S,:T,:U] for :SL2C
#                                [:Pp,:Qp,:Rp,:Sp,:Tp,:Up] for :SL2C_dot
# Must group dummies by vbundle before renaming.
```

## Design constraint

The key invariant is that _analyze_indices already groups by (name, vbundle),
so no changes are needed for dummy DETECTION. Only fresh name GENERATION and
display normalization need spinor awareness.

## Ground truth

- Penrose & Rindler Vol 1 (1984), Section 2.5: index conventions
- The SL(2,C) bundle has dim=2, so spinor indices range over {0,1}
- An undotted index A contracts only with another undotted index A (same vbundle)
- A dotted index A' contracts only with another dotted index A' (same vbundle)

## Tests

1. free_indices of Tensor(:psi, [spin_down(:A)]) returns [spin_down(:A)]
2. dummy_pairs of psi^A chi_A (product with spin_up(:A) and spin_down(:A) on :SL2C)
   correctly identifies the pair
3. No cross-bundle pairing: spin_up(:A, :SL2C) and spin_down(:A, :SL2C_dot) are
   both free, NOT a dummy pair
4. fresh_index(used; vbundle=:SL2C) returns :A if :A not in used
5. Mixed expression with :Tangent and :SL2C indices: each group analyzed independently","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tobias.osborne@itp.uni-hannover.de","created_at":"2026-03-15T10:20:41Z","created_by":"tobiasosborne","updated_at":"2026-03-18T20:01:02Z"}

{"id":"TGR-794","title":"Spinors: Implement spinor metric epsilon_{AB} with antisymmetric contraction","description":"## Summary

Register the SL(2,C) spin metric epsilon_{AB} and its inverse epsilon^{AB},
plus the dotted conjugates epsilon_{A'B'} and epsilon^{A'B'}. These are the
fundamental index-raising/lowering objects for spinor calculus, analogous to
g_{ab} for tensor indices but ANTISYMMETRIC.

Key difference from tensor metric: epsilon_{AB} = -epsilon_{BA}, so raising/
lowering picks up signs (the "see-saw" identity). The contraction engine in
contraction.jl must handle this antisymmetric metric correctly.

## Tensors to register

```julia
# In src/spinors/spin_metric.jl (~100 LOC)

define_spin_metric!(reg::TensorRegistry; manifold::Symbol=:M4)
```

Registers:
1. :eps_spin -- rank (0,2) on :SL2C, symmetry [AntiSymmetric(1,2)], is_metric=true
2. :eps_spin -- inverse: same tensor, rank (2,0), handled by metric engine (g^{AB} = epsilon^{AB})
3. :eps_spin_dot -- rank (0,2) on :SL2C_dot, symmetry [AntiSymmetric(1,2)], is_metric=true
4. :delta_spin -- rank (1,1) on :SL2C, is_delta=true (epsilon^{AC} epsilon_{CB} = delta^A_B)
5. :delta_spin_dot -- rank (1,1) on :SL2C_dot, is_delta=true

Store in registry metric_cache: metric_cache[:SL2C] = :eps_spin,
delta_cache[:SL2C] = :delta_spin (keyed by vbundle symbol, not manifold).
This requires extending metric_cache/delta_cache to also index by vbundle.

## Contraction engine changes

The existing _try_metric_contraction in contraction.jl already checks
idx.vbundle match, but uses metricQ=1 (symmetric) for dummy pair handling.
For spinor metrics, metricQ must be -1 (antisymmetric). The contraction
engine needs to check whether the metric tensor is antisymmetric and pass
the correct sign.

Key identity: epsilon^{AC} epsilon_{CB} = delta^A_B (NOT -delta^A_B).
This works because the contraction produces eps^{AC} eps_{CB} where the
matched indices are (C_up, C_down) and the free index A transfers normally.

## Ground truth

- Penrose & Rindler Vol 1 (1984), Eq 2.5.2-2.5.8:
  epsilon_{AB} = -epsilon_{BA}
  epsilon_{01} = 1, epsilon_{10} = -1
  epsilon^{AB} epsilon_{BC} = delta^A_C
  phi^A = epsilon^{AB} phi_B (raising convention: FIRST index of epsilon)
  phi_A = phi^B epsilon_{BA} (lowering convention: SECOND index of epsilon)

- Wald Ch 13, Eq 13.1.14-13.1.16

## Tests

1. epsilon_{AB} = -epsilon_{BA} after canonicalize
2. epsilon^{AC} epsilon_{CB} simplifies to delta^A_B
3. Self-trace: epsilon^A_A = epsilon^{AB} delta_{BA} = 2 (dim of SL2C bundle)
4. Sign: epsilon^{AB} epsilon_{AB} = 2 (not -2; contraction of antisym with antisym)
5. Dotted metric: same tests with :SL2C_dot indices
6. No cross-contraction: epsilon^{AB} (undotted) does not contract with phi_{A'} (dotted)","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tobias.osborne@itp.uni-hannover.de","created_at":"2026-03-15T10:21:06Z","created_by":"tobiasosborne","updated_at":"2026-03-18T20:00:25Z"}

new_value: Implemented with 3-agent workflow + reviewer. Spin metric epsilon with antisymmetric sign tracking in contraction engine. Penrose-Rindler convention verified. 29 tests. Commit a01556f.
